  1. All terminals are considered uninitialized at the beggining of a simulation cycle, not regarding values from the previous cycle
  2. Once a terminal is initialized, it stays initialized until the end of the statement block that it belongs to, or until the first jump statement
  3. After a jump statement, a terminal is initialized if it was initialized on all paths from which the point is reachable
  4. After if statement, a terminal is initialized if it was initialized prior to the statement or is initialized in all branches and if contains final else statement
  5. After for loop proven to be executed at least once, a terminal is initialized if it was initialized prior to the statement or it is initialized on all possible loop exits
  6. for loop is proven to be executed at least once if range expression contains only number literals, namespace variables, constant properties or attributes as direct subexpressions (i.e. 0:t.out.len, not (1 + 2):t.out.len) and range is properly formed (i.e. start value is smaller than end value)
  7. continue, break and the last statement in loop's statement block (if continue and break are omitted or if otherwise reachable) are considered as possible loop exits
  8. After for loop not proven to be executed at least once, a terminal is initialized only if was initialized before the statement
  9. After while loop, a terminal is initialized only if was initialized before the statement
  10. Every function call is a separate context that can both observe and alter different terminal initialization state
  11. After a function call, a terminal is initialized if it was initialized prior to the function call or if it is initialized on all possible function exits
  12. Explicit return statements and the last statement in function (if return is optional and omitted or if otherwise reachable) are considered as function exits
  13. In expressions, subexpressions that follow a function call in evaluation order see terminal initialization state as a side-effect

Warning

A common pattern of usage is that all terminals in an array are initialized in a for loop. This was the reason for introducing partial initialization and loop execution check. Such permissiveness can cause bugs if used without special care. TML users must pay special attention or resort to using full initialization.

Following examples show how terminal initialization analysis works.

fn output_fnc():
    e = t.out + 1 # t.out not initialized,
                  # error is raised

    t.out[1] = 1


    a = t.out + 2 # t.out partially initialized,
                  # only warning is raised

    t.out = 2

    b = t.out + 3 # t.out fully initialized
end

fn output_fnc():
    if a < b:
        t.out = 1
    end

    e = t.out + 1 # t.out not initialized,
                  # error is raised
end

fn output_fnc():
    if a < b:
        t.out = 1
    else:
        t.out = 2
    end

    e = t.out + 1 # t.out fully initialized
end

fn output_fnc():
    # static range
    for i=0:10:
        t.out[i] = 1
    end

    a = t.out + 1 # t.out partially initialized,
                  # a warning is raised

    # static range
    for i=0:10:
        t.out = 1
    end

    b = t.out + 1 # t.out fully initialized
end

fn output_fnc():
    # dynamic range
    for i=a:b:
        t.out[i] = 1
    end

    b = t.out + 1 # t.out not initialized,
                  # error is raised
end

fn output_fnc():
    while a < b:
        t.out = 1
    end

    e = t.out + 1 # t.out not initialized,
                  # error is raised
end

docs.title: Type system
docs.text: TML has support for basic and tensor types. Basic types represent scalar values (numbers and booleans) and strings. Tensor types represent multidimensional objects holding values of basic or tensor type.

docs.title: Basic types
docs.text:
• Numeric data types:
    • int - Signed integer numbers
    • uint - Unsigned integer numbers
    • real - Real numbers
  • bool - Logical values true and false
  • str - A string of characters
  • char - Single character string

Note

Relation between str and char types is similar to relation between tensor and scalar types, with infinite indexing of char possible. For more details, see section Index Access Broadcasting

Note

Using str and char as variable, parameter, function return or tensor base type in user code is not allowed. str and char are special types reserved for receiving configuration data from model scope.

docs.title: Integer
docs.text:
Integers can be defined in a binary, decimal or hexadecimal notation. For better readability, digits may be partitioned by the underscore separator _, which is ignored by the TML compiler.

Example:

a = 0b11    # binary notation
a = 42      # decimal notation
a = 0x2A    # hexadecimal notation


a = 100_000         # same as 100000
a = 100_000.134     # same as 100000.134

docs.title: Real
docs.text:
Real literals may be given in a x.y or scientific (exponential) notation.

Example:

real a = 2.5
real a = 1.05e-1

Shorthand notation where zeroes are left out (e.g. .3 or 2.) is not allowed.

docs.title: Tensor types
docs.text:
Tensor types are used to represent arrays, matrices, and general multidimensional objects. Tensor types allow for arbitrary composition of basic and tensor types.

Important

Tensors are statically allocated and their size cannot be changed during the runtime.

docs.title: Literals
docs.text:
TML supports literals for arrays, matrices and cubes. Row vectors are regarded as a special case of matrices. Literals can be nested to form objects consisting of other tensors, but the limitation is that dimension of one object cannot be larger than 3. For creating objects with dimension higher than 3, other mechanisms should be used.

Following code sample shows basic usage of tensor literals.

# 1D tensor (array)
a = [1, 2, 3]

# 2D tensor (matrix)
b = [1, 2, 3;
     1, 2, 3]

# 3D tensor (cube)
c = [1, 2, 3;
     1, 2, 3 |
     1, 2, 3;
     1, 2, 3]

Following code sample shows usage of nested tensor literals.

arr_of_arrays = [
    [1, 2],
    [1, 2],
    [1, 2]
]

cube_of_arrays = [
    [1], [2];
    [1], [2] |
    [1], [2];
    [1], [2]
]

Following code sample shows usage of arbitrary expressions in tensor literals.

arr = [1, 2, 3]

arr_of_arrays = [
    arr + 1,
    arr + 2,
    arr + 3
]

docs.title: Type declaration
docs.text: Type declaration enables explicit assignment of type to a variable that is not dependent on the result of type inference.

docs.title: Basic type
docs.text:
Variables of a basic type are declared using type keywords in declaration statements.

int a = 1
uint b = 2
real c = 1.5
bool d = true

docs.title: Tensor type
docs.text:
Variables of tensor types are declared using tensor type constructor in declaration statements. General form of tensor type constructor is tensor<type, len_per_dimension>.

Following example shows usage of tensor type constructor:

# int array of length 3
tensor<int, 3> a = [1, 2, 3]

# int array of length 3, with broadcasting applied
tensor<int, 3> a = 1

# int cube of 2x2x2 elements
tensor<int, 2, 2, 2> a = [
    1, 2;
    3, 4 |
    1, 2;
    3, 4
]

# array of two arrays
tensor<tensor<int, 2>, 2> a = [
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
]

docs.title: Derived type
docs.text:
Sometimes, a variable type depends on a terminal, or a property of the component. Derived type declaration enables declaring a variable of the same type as another one, with type changing according to terminal or property configuration.

Operator <variable>.type is used for derived type declaration. Types used in declaration statements can be derived only from variables from model scope.

Following example shows usages of derived type declaration:

# a is of input terminal's type
t.in.type a = 1

# b is of a's type
a.type b = 2

# c is of in_operand single element's type
t.in_operand[].type c = 3

docs.title: Type inference
docs.text:
TML is a statically typed language, but with support for type inference. If the variable type is omitted, the TML compiler will statically infer the type based on the initializer expression operands' types.

For basic arithmetic operations, scalar part of type is inferred as the more general type of operand scalar types.

For exponentiation, base scalar type is always real, as. Explicit casting is needed if int type is the desired result type.

Tensor type is inferred per Broadcasting rules.

If both operands are optional, inferred type is optional. If one operand is optional, and the other one is not, inferred type is not optional. Concrete types contained in optional expressions are inferred using ordinary type inference rules.

Example:

a = 10              # type is int
b = (a * 10) / 2    # type is int
a = 5u - 6u         # type is uint and overflow is present
b = 3 ** -2         # special case of type inference, type is real

TML compiler prefers inference to a more general type.

a = true + 1u       # type is uint
b = a + 1           # type is int
c = b * 2.0         # type is real

docs.title: Type casting
docs.text:
Type casting is necessary for evaluating expression with subexpressions of different types. Type casting can cause data loss when a source value cannot be represented in the target type (i.e. casting a real to an int).

Note

Usage of .type or any of the builtin cast operators results in creating a copy of the argument of the corresponding type. For example, int(arg) results in creating a copy of the arg of type int. Consider this while casting tensors.

docs.title: Implicit casting
docs.text:
In expressions, implicit type casting is automatically performed when casting from a smaller to a greater type (i.e. casting an int to a real), since no data loss can occur.

Note

We say that the type is smaller if all of its values can be represented in the bigger type.

Warning

Data loss can occur even when converting from int to real depending on the size of the target platform types. E.g., if for the target architecture both integer and real/double are 8 bytes there will be integer values that cannot be represented in the double type.

See warning section on this page.

We should be aware of this when developing code generators for various platforms. For now, we assume that TML int is smaller than TML real type for all current targets.

In assignments, implicit type casting is performed if storing a value to a variable would not cause data loss (i.e. storing an int value in a real variable).

Implicit type casting is related to type inference, as the type of the expression is inferred from operand and subexpression types. When the type of the expression is inferred, operands that are not of the inferred type are implicitly cast to the result type.

docs.title: Explicit casting
docs.text:
In expressions, explicit type casting is required when casting greater type to a smaller type (i.e. casting a real to an int), since data loss can occur. This makes programmer aware of potentially unwanted effects when writing and reading TML code.

Values can be cast using either type constructors (for both basic and tensor types) and using types derived from other variables. Derived type used in cast expression can be derived from any variable visible at the enclosing scope at that point.

docs.title: Casting to a basic type
docs.text:
The TML language supports the following operators for casting numeric values:

  • bool(<expr>) - returns the bool representation of <expr>
  • uint(<expr>) - returns the uint value constructed from <expr>
  • int(<expr>) - returns the int value constructed from <expr>
  • real(<expr>) - returns the real value constructed from <expr>
a = bool(123)        # a is 1u
b = bool(-123)       # b is 1u
c = bool(0.0)        # c is 0u

d = uint(true)       # d is 1u
e = uint(false)      # e is 0
f = uint(-123)       # f is 4294967173u because of overflow
g = uint(-123.13)    # g is 4294967173u because of overflow
h = uint(123.13)     # h is 123u

i = int(4294967173)  # i is -123 because of be overflow
j = int(-123.78)     # j is -123
k = int(123.78)      # k is 123

l = real(true)       # l is 1.0
r = real(-12344)     # m is -12344.0

Danger

The following expressions i = int(4294967173) # i is -123 becaue of overflow is unsupported at the moment. The advice is to take care of overflows.

docs.title: Casting to a tensor type
docs.text:
Casting to a tensor type is performed similarly to casting to a basic type. General form is <tensor_constructor>(<expr>).

a = tensor<int, 3, 3>(5)
b = tensor<tensor<int, 3, 3>, 3, 3>(5)

docs.title: Casting to a derived type
docs.text:
Operator <variable>.type(<expr>) can be used for converting <expr> to the type of the <variable>. If <variable> is of tensor type, operator <variable><[]>+.type(<expr>) allows declaring a variable of a single tensor element type.

Example:

a = 5
b = a.type(8.2)                 # 8.2 is casted to the type of `a` (`int`)
                                # and assigned to the `b` variable

c = t.in1.type(23)              # 23 is casted to the type of the `in1` terminal array
                                # and assigned to the `c` variable

d = t.in1[].type(25)             # 25 is casted to the type of the terminal array elements

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
Important: All assignments are done by value (implicit mem-copy).

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
a[1] = 0
c = b[1] == a[1]        # false

Warning

Assignments that use slicing (e.g. vec[1:5] = 3) are not supported at the moment.

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
Variables let you give names to values and refer to them later. The name of a variable must be a sequence of letters, digits, and underscores, but it may not begin with a digit. Every variable has an associated type (for more details, see Type system).

Example:

valid_variable_name = 0
a = 0
a2_1 = 0
_a_2 = 0

docs.title: Declaration
docs.text: In order to be used, variable needs to be declared. Variables can be declared using explicit or implicit declaration. Every variable belongs to one scope, and is visible in that scope and all child scopes.

docs.title: Explicit declaration
docs.text:
Explicit declaration is used to declare a new variable in the scope enclosing declaration statement. Expression in declaration statement and later uses of variable must respect declared type.

General form of explicit declaration:

<type> <name> = <expr>

Examples:

real a = 1
real b = 2.0
bool c = false
int d = 5.0    # TypeError: Expected int or type convertible to int, but received real

docs.title: Implicit declaration
docs.text:
Implicit declaration can be used as a shorter alternative to explicit declaration. Variable type is inferred from expression operands. Later assignments must respect type inferred on implicit declaration.

In global scope, every assignment statement is treated as implicit declaration. In local scope, assignment statement is treated as implicit declaration if variable with specified name does not exist.

General form of implicit declaration:

<name> = <expr>

Examples:

a = 5.0     # real
b = false   # bool
c = 1 + 1.5 # real

#later uses
c = "test"  # TypeError: Expected real or type convertible to real, but got string

docs.title: Scopes
docs.text:
Variable names live in scopes. Scope represents a range of TML code where a variable name exists and refers to the same value. TML uses lexical scoping. TML divides scopes into global scope and local scope(s).

Global component scope contains variables defined at the top-most outer level. In addition, each statement that contains nested code block has its local scope. Every function has at least one local scope, defined by the function body block. Functions can also have additional child local scopes, if they contain nested block statements such as if and for. 

int a = 0           # global scope

fn init():

   int b = 5        # function local scope

   for i = 1:10:
       int c = 3    # for loop local scope
   end

end

docs.title: Global scope
docs.text:
Global scope is the parent scope of all local scopes. Variables declared in global scope can be accessed in all local scopes.

Redeclaration of variables is not allowed in global scope. Forward referencing of variables is not allowed. Forward referencing of functions is allowed.

Variables in global scope can be declared either using explicit or implicit declaration. Every occurrence of variable assignment is considered as implicit declaration.

Functions that can be called in global scope are: - built-in functions (see Built-ins) - custom functions that are pure (i.e. do not depend on nor change global state) - custom functions that are guaranteed to terminate (i.e. do not contain while)

int a = 1 # explicit declaration
b = 2.0   # implicit declaration

a = 3     # Error: redeclaration of 'a'

x = y     # Error: 'y' is undeclared
y = 5

docs.title: Local scope
docs.text:
Explicit declaration creates a new variable in the scope enclosing the declaration statement. Assignment statement is treated as implicit declaration if a variable with the specified name does not exist. Assigning to a variable that exists in the outer scope will reference the existing variable instead.

To create a new variable with the same name in the current scope, variable must be declared using explicit declaration.

int a = 5

fn init():

   a = 10    # This references global 'a'

   # If we want variable by the same name we have to use declaration syntax
   int a = 10       # This variable is a new variable at the function level
   if true:
      a = 5         # This variable references function-level `a`
   else:
      int a = 3     # This is a new variable inside the `else` branch
   end

end

docs.title: Namespaces
docs.text:
TML has a set of special variables which can be accessed using t, p and n namespace. Variables from namespace can be accessed using the dot notation (for example t.in1).

Variables in t, p and n namespaces are predefined and cannot be redefined. Variables from the t namespace can be used to access variables generated from components terminals, the p namespace variable is similarly used to access component's property values, whereas the n namespace variable is used to access the values from the component's namespace. Example:

Assuming a component has:

  • three terminals: out (direction out), and in1 and in2 (both with direction in)
  • a property named mul_factor, and
  • a namespace variable named div_factor.

we can write:

t.out = ((t.in1 + t.in2) * p.mul_factor) / n.div_factor

Variables from built-in namespaces cannot be redeclared by the user.

Variables generated from the input terminals and properties cannot be written to (i.e. cannot be lvalues).

Variables generated from the output terminals must be initialized inside output_fnc before the first reference on the right-hand side of the statement.

Example:

fn output_fnc():
    a = t.out              # ValueError - Cannot use out terminal before initialization.
    t.out = t.in1          # valid - This statement is considered as the proper initialization
                           #         of the output terminal that can be safely used afterward.
    t.in1 = 5              # TypeError, input terminals are not lvalue
    p.mul_factor = 5       # TypeError
    n.div_factor = 5       # TypeError
    t.out += t.in1         # valid - The output terminal is properly initialized and can be used.
                           #         inside in-place assignment.
    t.out = t.out + t.in1  # valid - The output terminal is properly initialized and can be used
                           #         as rvalue.
end

Using terminal variables outside of the output_fnc is considered as an error. 

fn init_fnc():
    t.out = t.in    # ValueError - Cannot use terminal variables outside
                    #              `output_fnc`
end

Note, one can use the terminal variables inside functions called from the output_fnc. 

fn output_fnc():
    setup_output_terminal()
end

fn setup_output_terminal():
    t.out = 1  # The enclosing function is called from the `output_fnc`
               # so the output terminal can be used.
end

However, if a function manipulating with an output terminal variable is called from output_fnc and another function not called from the output_fnc, then the TML compiler raises an error. See the following example for more information. 

fn output_fnc():
    setup_output_terminal()
end

fn some_other_fnc():
    setup_output_terminal()  # TML reports an error because the
                             # function changing output terminal is called
                             # from the function different that `output_fnc`.
end

fn setup_output_terminal():
    t.out = 1
end

Note

If not stated differently, the namespace refers to the TML namespace (t, p, or n, respectively).

docs.title: Theoretical background
docs.text:
AST threading is the process of transforming abstract syntax tree (AST) to control flow graph (CFG). AST is an in-memory representation of program code created using parser. It is shaped by the grammar of the language. Parent nodes are named after their corresponding grammar production names. Node children correspond to production body children, and rules are applied recursively.

While AST representation is suitable for representing text, applying semantic analyses related to types and attaching translation rules for generating code, it is not suitable for static analysis related to dynamic behavior of programs. Analyzing control flow from such simple static structure is not possible.

By transforming static tree structure (AST) into a graph structure that approximates dynamic behavior (CFG) implementing control flow analyses becomes possible. It is said that CFG approximates dynamic program behavior because instead of deducing exact paths taken by a program execution, all theoretically possible paths are considered valid.

CFG is constructed by traversing AST in a mostly left-to-right depth-first manner, with exceptions for control flow statements that require special handling logic. CFG must be constructed using execution semantics of the programming language. Any malformations in regard to execution semantics can yield incorrect analysis results. Such bugs can either cause incorrect later optimization transformations (if CFG is used for optimization) or allow compilation of incorrect programs (if used for semantic analysis).

Warning

Theoretically, CFG can be more conservative than neccessary with soundness property still being satisfied. For a successful practical implementation, conservativeness must be defined in regards to programming language execution semantics.

On the other hand, accidentally conservative approximations, while not as dangerous for correctness, are still a problem as debugging such implementations can be confusing. This is why conservativeness must be properly specified and documented.

docs.title: TML implementation
docs.text:
TML program global scope contains variable declarations (implicit and explicit form) and function definitions. Function definitions contain statements and expressions. Functions can call other functions, and for purposes of AST threading and later DFA analyses, function calls are inlined.

AST threading is performed in three main phases: 1. Intraprocedural AST threading: Global scope definitions and function bodies are traversed 2. Interprocedural AST threading: Global scope definitions are connect with appropriate entry functions to create a graph that models simulation execution 3. Function call inlining: Function call nodes are replaced with copies of corresponding function bodies

As CFG is a structure that represents execution order of AST nodes, AST nodes can be used as CFG nodes with little to no modification. Implementations can choose whether a separate structure is needed or CFG can be represented over an existing AST. Current TML implementation uses existing AST nodes, with separate links between nodes that represent AST and CFG. A separate node is used as a placeholder for CFG start, as AST and CFG do not have the same start node. While start node of AST is always a node that is an instance of the start symbol from the grammar, with node children having the order they appear in program source code, CFG start node and node order is determined by program execution semantics.

docs.title: Global scope
docs.text:
Global scope contains variable declarations and function definitions. Both can appear in an arbitrary order in the global scope. As CFG must represent program execution order (which is not possible with AST), variables are traversed before function definitions. Variables are traversed in the order they were declared in the global scope, not regarding function defintions. Function defitions are traversed in the order they appear, not regarding variables.

Note that at the first stage on AST threading full simulation cycle is not formed. Function CFGs are not connected anywhere before cycle is formed.

docs.title: Entry functions and simulation cycle
docs.text:
After CFG is formed for global scope and individual functions, pseudo-while loop is created to represent CFG of component simulation cycle. Loop is called pseudo-while because it does not exist in TML code directly, but is needed as it is later generated by the compiler when the model is compiled. Note that this approach is simplified, as simulation cycle of complete model contains all components. Such simplified model is sufficient for single component analyses and optimizations.

Following images show AST of a program with all entry functions and corresponding CFG after creation of pseudo-while loop. Note that while function definitions of entry functions are inlined, calls to other functions are not yet inlined. From this point forward, functions that are not called by global scope expressions or entry functions (either directly or indirectly) are not present in CFG. As all entry functions are optional as long as all terminals are initialized at the end of simulation cycle, entry functions not present in AST are omitted in CFG.

docs.title: Expressions
docs.text:
Expressions are threaded using generic left-to-right depth-first traverasal of the AST. AST is shaped by the grammar rules of associativity and operator precedence.

Following images show an example of expression AST and corresponding CFG. Expressions that consist of other expressions are illustrated as subtrees with all children for readability. They are serialized in text dumps in tests the same way.

docs.title: Statements
docs.text:
Unlike expressions, statements cannot be threaded using generic traversal logic, as such traversal would not represent multiple possible execution paths.

When traversing AST, special logic is triggered for statements. Manual handlers create edges that appropriately represent possible execution paths. Additionally, manual handlers introduce helper nodes not present in AST. Helper nodes make later CFG traversal more convenient and can hold additional metadata. Helper nodes also make writing special-case transfer functions easier.

Two main types of helper nodes are start and join nodes. Start nodes represent beginning of control flow statements and function definitions. Join nodes represent end of control flow statements that have multiple possible execution paths. In case of loops, concepts of start and join nodes can overlap. Join nodes have multiple incoming edges but only one outgoing edge. Join nodes have associated join functions that combine inputs into a single output.

Note

Concepts of join functions and join nodes should not be confused. For purposes of many data flow analyses (such as reaching definition analysis), join function is applied on all nodes that have multiple inputs. If those nodes happen to have an associated transfer function, it is applied to joined input state. Concrete implementation details and relation between transfer and join functions are explained in documentation for corresponding analyses.

Note

Special case transfer functions are needed in some analyses, mainly terminal initialization analysis. Unlike simpler analyses such as reaching definition analysis, where there is only one transfer and join function, more complex analyses can have multiple rules depending on the node type. This is a concept relatively unique to TML implementation.

docs.title: If
docs.text: Following images show corresponding CFGs of an if, if/else and if/elseif/else statements. if statement has one start node (if node) and one join node (join_if node). As else and elseif clauses are optional, they are potentially not present in CFG. if/elseif sequences are threaded as if/else with new if neseted inside else statement block.

docs.title: For
docs.text:
Following image shows CFG of a for loop without break statements. range and iter_var are traversed before entering the loop as corresponding expressions are evaluated only once before entering the loop.

for is a helper node that acts both as a start and join node. End of loop body is connected to for node as expression is not evaluated on every loop iteration. While iteration variable value is updated on every iteration, using current approach is sufficient for all practical purposes. for node makes later traversal of CFG easier by making it explicit that multiple outgoing paths need to be traversed.

for_end is a helper end node that acts as a join node also, as it can have multiple entry paths if break statement is used.

Additionally, for end keeps link to last node of statement block, which may be needed for non-standard analyses such as terminal initialization analysis. Statement block is not connected to for_end directly because if no jump statements are used, for loop is never directly exited before checking loop condition.

docs.title: While
docs.text:
Following image shows CFG of a while loop without break statements. Having separate while and while_body helper nodes is not a theoretical requirement, but is necessary for easier CFG traversal as terminal analysis requires special join function for loop start, and determining whether an arbitrary expression node belongs to a while is not possible directly.

while is a helper node that acts both as a start and join node. End of loop body is connected to while node as expression is evaluated on every loop iteration.

while_body is a helper start node that makes later traversal of CFG easier by making it explicit that multiple outgoing paths need to be traversed.

while_end is a helper end node that acts as a join node also, as it can have multiple entry paths if break statement is used.

Statement block is not connected to while_end directly because if no jump statements are used, while loop is never directly exited before checking loop condition.

docs.title: Break
docs.text:
break statement stops execution of a loop at a point and exits immedately. Following TML execution semantics, break gets connected directly to end node of the corresponding loop. Following image shows CFGs of break if for and while loops respectively.

Warning

Red edge represents a bug in AST threading that will be solved in the future.

docs.title: Continue
docs.text:
continue statement stops execution of a loop at a point and starts a new loop iteration, if loop condition is satisfied after evaluation. Following TML execution semantics, continue gets connected directly to start node of the corresponding loop. In the case of while loop, continue is connected to while and not to while_body as continue requires evaluation of loop condition. Following image shows CFGs of continue if for and while loops respectively.

Warning

Red edge represents a bug in AST threading that will be solved in the future.

docs.title: Return
docs.text:
return statement enables either returning a value in non-void functions, finishing execution before the last statement, or both. Following TML execution semantics, return gets connected directly to func_exit node. Follwing image shows CFG of a function with return statement.

Warning

Red edge represents a bug in AST threading that will be solved in the future.

docs.title: Guard blocks
docs.text:
Guard blocks have mandatory main branch and optional else branch. Unlike if which is considered as a dynamic control flow statement (in general case, not considering possibility of static evaluation and pruning), guard block branches are selected and pruned at compile time.

Following image shows CFG for guard blocks in cases in which either main or else branch is selected. If no branch is selected (in cases of unreachable blocks or blocks without else), whole guard block is discarded from CFG. guard_main and guard_else are helper start nodes. guard_end is a helper end node. Note that guard_end is not a join node, as it has only one entry.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Following image shows CFG of a function before inlining. Helper node func_entry is added at the beginning of function CFG, and func_exit is added at the end. Rest of the function body is threaded using rules for statements and expressions. Function arguments are currently not connected to the CFG.

Note

While function arguments are not traversed and connected to CFG, argument types and return type are traversed and connected. This does not cause any problems as those node types do not have associated transfer functions. This will be changed in the future.

docs.title: Function inlining
docs.text:
Following image shows function call before and after inlining. As parameters are expressions that do have side effects and associated transfer functions, they are traversed before function call with all rules applied (including function call inlining).

Function calls are inlined by making a copy of original function CFG and by inserting it in the place of previous function_call node, which gets replaced. CFG must be copied as different function calls are considered as different instances of inlining.

Function inlining in CFG is necessary for semantic analyses that handle function calls in different context separately, such as terminal initalization analysis. It also enables optimizations such as inlining and function specialization (this is not implemented currently).

docs.title: Broadcasting
docs.text:
Following sections describe formal rules of broadcasting applied in binary operations and assignments. Rules were formulated based on the following observations:

  1. When applying a binary operation or an assignment, types of operands must be compatible
  2. Types are considered compatible if they meet order relation imposed by the type hierarchy
  3. While total order relation is adequate for binary operations of scalar types, it can be inappropriate for tensors
  4. Essential difference between assignment and binary operation rules are conditions which must be met in respect to the type hierarchy
  5. For an assignment \( a = b \) to be possible, condition \( T_b \le T_a \) must be met, i.e. types must be contained in an order relation
  6. For a binary operation \( c = a + b \) to be possible, condition \( \exists T_c, \: T_a \le T_c \wedge T_b \le T_c \) must be met, i.e. upper bound type must exist and operand types do not need to be in an order relation, but both operand types must be in an order relation with the resulting type

docs.title: Binary operations
docs.text:
Binary operation is possible on all types for which an upper bound exists.

\( \forall A, B \: \exists C, \: A \le C \wedge B \le C \)

Scalar types are contained a total order relation, and their upper bound is the greater type (ex. \( real \vee int = real \)). For tensor types, broadcasting rules are checked and applied recursively until a base scalar type is reached.

For tensor types to have an upper bound, tensor base type must have an upper bound and dimension conditions must be met.

\( \exists T_C, \: T_A \le T_C \wedge T_B \le T_C \ \)

The number of dimensions of both tensors must be equal. If it is not equal, tensor with the smaller number of dimensions is expanded with dimensions with the length of 1 to the left (ex. \( 3\times3 \to 1\times1\times1\times3\times3 \)).

\( ndim = ndim(A) = ndim(B)\)

All corresponding dimensions of both tensors must either be the same or one of them must equal to 1. Dimensions that are equal to 1 are expanded the length of the greater corresponding dimension.

\( \forall i\in \mathbb{N}, i \le ndim \)

\( lendim_i(A) = lendim_i(B) \vee lendim_i(A) = 1 \vee lendim_i(B) = 1 \)

Note

Broadcasting in binary operations is attempting to create a new tensor type, and then convert both operand to the newly created type and then apply the operation.

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
For an assignment to be possible, types must be contained in an order relation and the type of the RHS must be smaller than the type of the LHS (ie. RHS type must be convertible to the LHS type).

\( a = b, \: T_b \le T_a \)

Scalar type is convertible to types that are greater in the type hierarchy (ex. \( int \le real \)). For tensor types, broadcasting rules are checked and applied recursively until a base scalar type is reached.

For a tensor type \( T_b: tensor \left\langle T_{base_b} \right\rangle \) to be convertible to \( T_a: tensor \left\langle T_{base_a} \right\rangle \), tensor base type must be convertible and dimension conditions must be met.

\( T_{base_b} \le T_{base_a} \)

Number of dimensions of the RHS must be less than or equal to the number of dimensions on the LHS. In contrast to the binary operation broadcasting, broadcasting is applied only to the RHS. RHS tensor cannot be broadcast to exceed LHS dimensions, and LHS type cannot be altered in any way.

\( ndim(B) \le ndim(A) \)

The number of dimensions of both tensors must be equal after RHS expansion. RHS is expanded with dimensions with the length of 1 to the left (ex. \( 3\times3 \to 1\times1\times1\times3\times3 \)).

\( ndim(B_{expanded}) = ndim(A) \)

After expansion, broadcasting is performed. Length of the corresponding dimensions must either be equal, or the length of RHS dimension should be equal to 1. RHS dimensions with the length of 1 are expanded to the length of the corresponding LHS dimension.

\( lendim_i(B) = lendim_i(A) \vee lendim_i(B) = 1 \)

```admonish note While broadcasting in binary operation can alter both operands to produce a new upper bound type, broadcasting in assignments is more restrictive.

Broadcasting in assignments, in contrast, is attemting to reshape RHS to be of the same type as LHS. Such transformation is possible only if LHS does not need to be reshaped, or have it's base type altered in any way. ```

docs.title: Function call graph analysis
docs.text:
Function call graph is a directed graph in which functions are represented as nodes, and calls are represented as edges. Edges are directed from the caller to the callee.

Indegree of a node N represents the total number of functions calling function N. Outdegree of a node N represents the total number of functions called by function N.

Loops in the call graph indicate the presence of recursion. Recursion can be either direct (when a function is calling itself directly) or indirect (when a function is calling itself through other functions). Direct recursion is recognized when a direct link of node with itself is present in the call graph. Indirect recursion is recognized when a path to the node exists, but through traversing other nodes in the call graph.

If loops are present, call graph is an arbitrary directed graph and has no topological sort. If loops are not present, call graph is a directed acyclic graph and has a corresponding topological sort.

Recursion is forbidden because in the general case, inlining recursive calls is not possible. A valid TML program must have a call graph that is a DAG.

DAGs cannot be traversed using plain BFS/DFS algorithms, because multiple entry nodes exist, and having a node already traversed does not indicate a cycle. For traversing the call graph, detecting cycles and obtaining a topological sort, three color DFS algorithm is used.

Topological sort of call graph is used in effect analysis because traversing functions in a single pass is simpler than using a worklist implementation.

docs.title: Code pruning and loop unrolling
docs.text:
In this phase we shall use DFA information to prune code that is proven unreachable for the given input model. For example, if the condition in the if/else is statically resolvable we can calculate it at compile time and replace this statement with the only block that will ever be executed (either the body of if or else). This serves to support alternative implementation based on user-configurable inputs from the model etc.

The same principle is applied to loop unrolling where we must unroll loops whose counter variable is marked as must be static, i.e. loops that contain must be static expression in its body which depends on the counter variable.

```admonish note A must be static expressions must be evaluated at compile time. For example, TML strings are currently must be static as we shall use them only at compile time, i.e. they should not end-up in the generated code. 

When the loop counter range is static then we shall replace the loop with its
unrolled version. 

```admonish note
Unrolling shall be done if:
- loop count is static and loop contains _must be static_ expressions in its
  body with loop counter as its part, or
- loop count is static and smaller then configured unrolling threshold.

If a loop count contains _must be static_ where the loop counter is part of the expression but the counter is dynamic, it should be reported as an error.

Loop unrolling works in tandem with pruning so the inner code shall be pruned on each unroll iteration (because the inner if/else statements can depend on loop counter). Basically, we shall do for loop and if/else interpretation for statically resolvable expressions. This entails that we should recheck static/dynamic property of each inner expression on each iteration during unrolling and unroll/prune inner statements accordingly.

Also, on each pruning we shall remove code locations of the pruned code from all In/Out sets from DFA and recalculate static/dynamic expressions properties. The rationale for this is that some variable usages might become static after a code pruning step.

The process of code pruning, unrolling, and static values calculations shall be repeated using the work-list algorithm until nothing more can be statically calculated, pruned or unrolled.

Code pruning should always be done as soon as possible to avoid unnecessary processing of the code that will eventually be pruned.

At the end of the process all our if conditions must be dynamic, and all for loop bodies must not contain must be static expressions.

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
In preparation phase we do an AST walk in order to determine each variable definition (assignment) and usage (read) location. For this, we construct a form of symbol table where we have an entry for each variable in the program. Each entry holds additional meta-data required by the further analysis.

Since TML is lexically scoped this symbol table will take a shape of a tree where the nodes are scopes. Thus, the root of the tree is the global scope. During the AST walk, whenever we cross the beginning of the syntax block which can have local variables we create a new node in the symbol table tree.

For each variable assignment we record the location (e.g. a reference to assignment node) in the set of definitions for the given variable (a meta-data kept in the entry of the symbol table). This set of definition will be used as a KILL set in the DFA (see bellow). For each variable usage (read) we record location in another set in the symbol table entry. This information can be used in other type of analysis (e.g. liveness).

As in the examples below, the symbol table might be represented as a lookup table that maps symbolic names of variables to the corresponding entries, each containing two sets, one with definition locations and another with read (use) locations of the matching variable. While doing an AST walk, at the moment of encountering the node that (re)defines a variable (the variable is on the LHS), we save a reference to the corresponding entry of the symbol table in the node. Similarly, when encountering a node that reads (use) the same variable (the variable is on the RHS), we save a reference to the matching entry in the node. Those references allow the direct access to the corresponding entries of the symbol table while doing the latter analyses without need to be aware of lexical scopes.

In this phase we shall also mark expressions that must be static. This analysis start from all expression that has a must be static constituents (currently strings). When an expression contains a string it is marked as must be static. All variables in the expression must be static also for the whole expression to remain static and thus all other expressions that contain one of these variables are must be static. This information propagates through expressions/variables using work-list algorithm. The must be static information is used after DFA for loop unroll and if statements pruning.

docs.title: DF execution
docs.text:
For the start we shall do Reachable Definitions Analysis (RDA) which is a form of Forward May analysis where for each variable usage point we calculate a set of definitions (assignment) which may influence the given location.

Or, to specify it more precise:

A definition of variable v reaches program point p if there exists a path with no intervening assignment to v

This is also called def-use information.

For DFA: - In is calculated as union of Out of all predecessors. - KILL information is found in the symbol table for the symbol at LHS of the assignment. It is a set of references to assignment locations collected in preparation phase. - GEN is a just a reference to the statement of the current assignment. - Out is calculated as Out = GEN U (In \ KILL)

Now, a standard DF worklist based algorithm is used until fixpoint is reached (a state in which new iteration does not change outputs). See [^grune2012], chapter 5 and [^foster2011] slide 26.

The result are In sets for each statement in the program (program point) which specifies reachable definitions, i.e. assignments that can influence the given statement. This can be used to determine for each variable usage if it can be statically evaluated and what will be the value.

[^grune2012]: Dick Grune, Kees Van Reeuwijk, Henri E Bal, Ceriel JH Jacobs, and Koen Langendoen. Modern compiler design. Springer Science & Business Media, 2012. [^foster2011]: Jeff Foster. Dataflow analysis. 2011. lecture notes.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: As @igord proposed, this file was created to serve as a sketchbook with initial thoughts about the development of the TML v0.2. Before any major coding, the TML developers should discuss about ideas through the sketchbook to avoid hacking in Python characteristic for so far TML development process.

docs.title: v0.2
docs.text:
The major novelty that TML v0.2 should bring is the comprehensive Data Flow Analysis (DFA). The plan is to enrich the TML compiler with Control Flow Graph that serves as a foundation for better understanding the dynamic behavior of TML programs. The chapter 5 of the book: "Modern Compiler Design" [^grune2012] serves as a holy book for TML v0.2 developers. They obliged to carefully study it with the goal to develop the stable data flow analyzer. Beside optimal analyzer they all strive to, the code quality should be the one the most important property.

The data flow analyzer consists of two major parts: - AST Threading - Data-flow Equations

docs.title: Multislicing
docs.text:
This section provides some ideas and explanations on the new multislicing operation.

Multislicing is an extension to slicing. Where slicing a tensor returns a sub-tensor, multislicing returns a tensor of tensors.

For example, let x be of type tensor<int, 3, 4> (i.e. a matrix).

Multislice expression:

x[:*, :]

would be of type tensor<tensor<int, 4>, 3>. E.g. each element of the resulting tensor will be a row from x or to be more specific the result would be [x[0, :], x[1, :], x[2, :]].

While:

x[:, :*]

would be of type tensor<tensor<int, 3>, 4>. E.g. each element of the resulting tensor will be a column from x or [x[:, 0], x[:, 1], x[:, 2], x[:, 3]]

Multislice can operate over slices with given boundaries. For example, these are all valid expressions and the resulting types:

  • x[:2*, :] - tensor<tensor<int, 4>, 2> ([x[0, :], x[1, :]])
  • x[:, 1:3*] - tensor<tensor<int, 3>, 2> ([x[:, 1], x[:, 2]])
  • x[1:, :2*] - tensor<tensor<int, 2>, 2> ([x[1:, 0], x[1:, 1]])

We could generalize multislicing to produce higher dimension outer tensor.

For example, let x be of type tensor<int, 3, 4, 5>. Then:

x[1:*, :, :*]

Would be of type tensor<tensor<int, 4>, 2, 5> or we get a matrix of vectors:

[
    x[1, :, 0], x[1, :, 1], x[1, :, 2], x[1, :, 3], x[1, :, 3] ;
    x[2, :, 0], x[2, :, 1], x[2, :, 2], x[2, :, 3], x[2, :, 3] ;
]

docs.title: A note of calling functions on tensors
docs.text:
TML functions operate either element-wise or over all scalar elements. If the inner elements are of tensor type functions are applied recursivelly.

For example:

  • sin(x) where x is of type tensor<float, 3, 4> would return the same type where on each tensor element sin operation is performed.
  • average(x) is of scalar type if inner type of x tensor is scalar. If inner type of x tensor is a tensor then the return will be a tensor of the same dimension as the outer tensor. For example:
x: tensor<float, 3, 4>
average(x) gives float

x: tensor<tensor<float, 2, 2>, 5>
average(x) gives tensor<float, 5> where average is applied to each inner tensor

docs.title: A note on relational operators
docs.text:
Relational operator is performed element-wise when applied to tensors. If the tensors are nested the operation is performed recursivelly. Thus, the return type would be the same dimension but with the bool final inner type.

For example:

Let x be of type tensor<float, 3, 4>:

Then:

x > 5

produces a value of type tensor<bool, 3, 4> where each element greater than 5 will produce true value.

docs.title: all and any
docs.text:
These are functions that operate over all elements of tensor and produce scalar if innter type is bool. If inner type is tensor it is applied recursively until reaching scalars of bool type. It is an error if these functions are applied to tensors whose final scalar type is not bool.

For example:

x: tensor<bool, 2, 3>

all(x) -> returns true if all elements are true
any(x) -> returns true if any element is true

x: tensor<tensor<bool, 2, 2>, 3>
all(x) -> returns tensor<bool, 3>

Some examples of condition tests:

if any(x > 10)    # if any element of x is greater than 10
    ...

if all(x < 0)    # if all elements of x are negative

docs.title: Combining multislicing and function calls
docs.text:
Multislicing is a powerfull operator which provides great flexibility.

Let x be of type tensor<float, 5, 4>.

To ask "is there any row with sin value greater than 0.5 we can write:

if any(all((sin(x) > 0.5)[:*, :])))

Lets break down this expression:

First we calculate sin for each element of tensor x. The result is of the same type as x. 

sin(x)

Then we compare the result to 0.5 and get tensor<bool, 5, 4> with true value for each element whose sin is greater than 0.5:

sin(x) > 0.5

Then we are apply multislice over rows (first dimension) thus getting tensor<tensor<float, 4>, 5> or a tensor of all rows:

(sin(x) > 0.5)[:*, :]

Now, we are applying all recursively (because we have tensor of tensors) to all inner tensors (rows) and we get a tensor of booleans - tensor<bool, 5>:

all((sin(x) > 0.5)[:*, :]))

At the and we apply any to tensor of booleans. This time any won't recurse as we have a tensor of scalars and we get a single boolean value:

any(all((sin(x) > 0.5)[:*, :])))

To ask the same question for columns we would just do a multislice over columns:

if any(all((sin(x) > 0.5)[:, :*])))

We can see that using slicing/multislicing, function call and relational operators rules provide a great flexibility and consistency. The rules work the same accross all functions and relational operators thus once learned can be applied in a consistent manner.

Note

any can be optimized to return early as soon as the first element which satisfied condition is found. But, this should not influence the language usage and should be left to compiler optimization.

[^grune2012]: Dick Grune, Kees Van Reeuwijk, Henri E Bal, Ceriel JH Jacobs, and Koen Langendoen. Modern compiler design. Springer Science & Business Media, 2012.

docs.title: Effect analysis
docs.text:
Effect system represents a way of complementing type system with information on what effects expression evaluation can cause. In TML, effects are used to determine which expressions can be statically evaluated, which functions are allowed to access terminals and which functions potentially do not terminate.

Supported effects are listed in the table below.

Effect Parameter Description READ_GLOBAL Variable A global variable was read WRITE_GLOBAL Variable A global variable was written READ_OUTPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context A terminal was read WRITE_OUTPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context A terminal was written to READ_FEEDTHROUGH_OUTPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context A feedthrough output terminal was read WRITE_FEEDTHROUGH_OUTPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context A feedthrough output terminal was written to READ_INPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context An input terminal was read READ_FEEDTHROUGH_INPUT Terminal,flag that denotes access in feedthrough guarded context An input terminal was written to READ_TUNABLE_PROPERTY Tunable property A tunable property was read GUARD_EXISTS Variable A variable was guarded using exists statement GUARD_NOT_EXISTS Variable A variable was guarded using not exists statement GUARD_FEEDTHROUGH Variable A variable was guarded using feedthrough statement GUARD_NOT_FEEDTHROUGH Variable A variable was guarded using not feedthrough statement NON_TERMINATING none A function cannot be proven to terminate ACCESS_UNGUARDED_OPTIONAL Variable An optional variable was accessed outside exists statement DERIVE_UNGUARDED_OPTIONAL Variable Type of an optional variable was derived outside exists statement

Effects can be stored at the expression level in the AST, ranging from the simplest forms (literals and variable references) to complex expressions. Current implementation stores effects at function level in a separate structure called effect scope.

Effects are not present in TML syntax and effect inference is performed automatically. Users are aware of effects only in the form of error messages such for actions such as forbidden terminal access.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Effect analysis is performed as an AST traversal, with separate passes for local and global scope.

Local scope analysis is performed before global scope analysis. Effects of functions are determined as the union of all effects caused directly by the function and by the callee functions.

Besides effect type and parameter variable, every effect holds guard context in which it was created. This enables detecting whether an effect is reachable in the context in which it is inherited. If it is reachable, it is inherited. Otherwise, it is not inherited and a warning is raised. Note that an effect might be reachable (and inherited) in one context, and unreachable (and discarded) in another.

Expressions in global scope must be statically evaluated, so only pure terminating functions can be called in global scope. A function is considered pure if it doesn't have any effects. A function is considered terminating if it is not recursive and does not have a while statement (either directly or in called code).

docs.title: Guard context
docs.text:
Guard context is defined either using guard blocks or inline guards. Guard context contains assumptions about variable properties that are valid in a certain portion of code. Properties of concern are presence of optional variables and feedthrough property value of dynamic feedthrough terminals. Guard context consists of 6 separate sets of assumptions, described in the table below. While behavior is same for both guard blocks and inline guards, sets are separate for easier readability of test and debug output.

Set Description guarded_exists Set of terminals guarded using exists statement guarded_not_exists Set of terminals guarded using not exists statement guarded_feedthrough Set of terminals guarded using feedthrough statement guarded_not_feedthrough Set of terminals guarded using not feedthrough statement inline_guarded_exists Set of terminals guarded using inline guard operator ? inline_guarded_not_exists Set of terminals guarded using inline guard operator ? in left elvis operator branch that are assumed not to be present in the right elvis operator branch

docs.title: Total function effects
docs.text:
Function definition is represented as a subtree of AST, beginning with function_definition node. Functions are analyzed in the topological order. Functions that call no functions have no inherited effects and can be analyzed first. Dependencies are then analyzed before the dependent functions.

If a simple expression with a certain known effect is encountered during AST traversal, the effect is added to the effect scope belonging to the functions. For example, a statement such as t.in = 1 would add WRITE_TERMINAL<t.in> effect.

If a function call is encountered, all reachable callee effects are inherited. An effect is considered reachable if it was created in a guard context that is not opposite to the guard context in which the function call is placed. Terminal effects are mapped to their guarded counterparts, if necessary.

Guard context is considered opposite if intersection of sets that hold opposite assumptions is not empty. For such guard contexts, it can be statically proved that nested context will not be reachable under any possible configuration. There are two pairs of opposite assumptions: exists/not exists and feedthrough/not feedthrough.

docs.title: Function effect inheritance
docs.text:
Inheritance of reachable effects is performed using following rules:

  1. If the effect is related to terminal access:
  2. If the terminal is guarded using feedthrough or not feedthrough in the context in which the function is called, it is mapped to the guarded equivalent of the effect and inherited
  3. Otherwise, the effect is directly inherited
  4. If the effect is ACCESS_UNGUARDED_OPTIONAL or DERIVE_UNGUARDED_OPTIONAL:
  5. If parameter variable is guarded using not exists in current context, an error is raised
  6. If parameter variable is guarded using exists in current context, the effect is discarded
  7. Otherwise, the effect is directly inherited
  8. Otherwise, the effect is directly inherited
  9. Guard context of inherited effect is union of original effect's guard context and the current guard context

Note

Only reachable effects are considered for inheritance, so it is sufficient to check if parameter variables of terminal effects are guarded in the current context using feedthrough or not feedthrough. As unreachable effects are discarded, only terminal effects for unguarded feedthrough access remain.

docs.title: AST traversal
docs.text: Effect analysis is performed as mostly left-to-right depth-first AST traversal. Guard context originating from guard blocks is stored in symbol table and is easy to track. Current type analysis supports only storing scopes per statement block, and does not support scopes per single statement or per single expression. Because of this, separate structure is needed to store inline guarded context. Ordinary order of AST traversal is broken when traversing elvis expressions. Right branch is traversed before left branch, because inline guarded not exists context can only be registered in preorder traversal of elvis expression, due to language grammar not having separate rules nodes for elvis expression branches. To prevent double traversal (as tree gets revisited in regualar traversal), sets of visited variables and function calls are maintained.

docs.title: Top-level function effects
docs.text:
After analyzing function effects, a check is performed for top-level effects.

Top-level functions are functions that are not called by other functions. TML defines special built-in functions (such as output_fnc). Only special built-in functions are allowed to have effects related to terminal access.

As top-level functions are dead code, and allowed terminal effects of custom functions cen be determined only in call context, determining allowed effects is not possible. A warning is raised that errors related to terminal access are ignored.

docs.title: Global scope
docs.text: Subset of the AST that consists of nodes that are not function definitions represents the global scope. Simple expressions with forbidden effects immediately raise an error. Function calls with forbidden effects raise an error.

docs.title: Simple linear CFG
docs.text:
The following snippet shows a simple function with three nodes (marked as N1-N3) forming linear CFG.

fn foo():
    int x = 5  # N1
    int y = 3  # N2
    x = y + 2  # N3
end

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
The first step is to walk the AST to build a one-level symbol table containing metadata about variables x and y. In this example, we consider only set of definitions of the variables and ignore set of read locations. The sets of definitions contain references to the nodes that define or assign to the corresponding variables. The symbol table content is the following

{
  x: {N1, N3};
  y: {N2};
}

In this step, we determine the KILL sets of all nodes. As described above, the GEN set of a node contains the reference to that node.

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
For each node, we calculate In and Out sets iteratively until reaching the fixed point.

## The first and only iteration
# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = OUT(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1, N2}

# N3
In(N3) = OUT(N2) = {N1, N2}
Out(N3) = {N2, N3}

docs.title: Static Values Calculation
docs.text: For each node (program point), we determine whether the variable modified by the node is static, undefined or dynamic. The node N1 defines variable x. Since its In is empty and the RHS of the definition uses only literals, x is marked as static. Similarly, the y is static too. The node N3 redefines the value of x by using y and literal. Using the In set of N3, we determine that only one definition of y (inside the node N2) influences the value of x, meaning the both y and x are static.

docs.title: The CFG with an if-statement
docs.text:
Consider the following snippet with if statement. Note that N4 points both to the N5 and N9.

fn foo():
    int x = 0       # N1
    int y = x + 1   # N2
    if x > y:       # N3 is condition, N4 is if_statement node
        x = x + 1   # N5
        int x = 2   # N6
        x = x + 5   # N7
        y = x + 2   # N8
    end             # N9 is join_if
    z = x + y       # N10
end

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
The symbol table has two nested scopes. The outer scope corresponds the foo function, while the nested scope matches the if branch. The first assignment to x inside the branch modifies the x of the outer scope, while the later definitions/assignment modifies the x of the inner scope. Finally, the symbol table has the following shape:

{
  x: {N1, N5};
  y: {N2, N8};
  z: {N10};
  {
    # The scope corresponds to the if-branch.
    x: {N6, N7}
  }
}

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
The N4 nas two successors: N5 and N9, so the process of calculating In and Out looks like this:

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1, N2}

# KILL(N3) = GEN(N3) = EMPTY
In(N3) = Out(N2) = {N1, N2}
Out(N3) = In(N3) = {N1, N2}

# KILL(N4) = GEN(N4) = EMPTY
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2}
Out(N4) = In(N4) = {N1, N2}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5}

# N6 
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N2, N5}
Out(N6) = {N2, N5, N6}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N2, N5, N6}
Out(N7) = {N2, N5, N7}

# N8 
In(N8) = Out(N7) = {N2, N5, N7}
Out(N8) = {N5, N7, N8}

# KILL(N9) = {N6, N7}, GEN(N9) = EMPTY
In(N9) = Out(N8) U Out(N4) = {N5, N7, N8} U {N1, N2} = {N1, N2, N5, N7, N8}
Out(N9) = {N1, N2, N5, N8}

# N10
In(N10) = Out(N9) = {N1, N2, N5, N8}
Out(N10) = {N1, N2, N5, N8, N10}

The join_if node is responsible to KILL all variables local for the if branch scope.

docs.title: Static Values Calculation
docs.text:
Considering the RHS of the first node, one concludes the x is static in N1. The same node influences the definition of y inside N2, so both variables are static at this program point. The static property of both variables are transferred to node N3 and N4. The assignment to x in node N5 depends on the value of x inside N1, meaning the x is static in N5. Trivially, one can notice that the x belonging to the inner scope is static in both nodes N6 and N7. Assignment to y inside the node N8 is influenced by the assignment to x of the inner scope in N7, meaning both variables are static inside this node.

Two assignments to the x of the outer corresponding the N1 and N5 nodes respectively influences join_if node N9, meaning the x is dynamic. The same stands for the y. Node N10 defines x by using the dynamic variable x and y, causing the z to become dynamic.

If the node N has exactly one predecessor and the N does not modify the variable var, then the var has the same property (undefined, static, or dynamic) as in the N's predecessor. This property was implicitly assumed while describing the Static Values Calculation for all examples.

docs.title: The CFG with an if-else-statement
docs.text:
The following code snippets shows a function containing if-else statement.

fn foo():
    int x = 0       # N1
    int y = x + 1   # N2
    if x > y:       # N3 is condition, N4 is if_statement node
        x += 1      # N5 is equivalent to `x = x + 1`
    else:
        x += 2      # N6 is equivalent to `x = x + 2`
        y = x + 2   # N7
    end             # N8 is join_if
    z = x + y       # N9
end

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
The preparation phase produces the following symbol table: 

{
  x: {N1, N5, N6};
  y: {N2, N7};
  z: {N9};
  {
    # No local variables in the if-branch scope.
  };
  {
    # No local variables in the else-branch scope.
  }
}

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
Reaching definitions analysis behaves as follows:

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1, N2}

# KILL(N3) = GEN(N3) = EMPTY
In(N3) = Out(N2) = {N1, N2}
Out(N3) = {N1, N2}

# KILL(N4) = GEN(N4) = EMPTY
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2}
Out(N4) = {N1, N2}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5}

# N6
In(N6) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N6) = {N2, N6}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N2, N6}
Out(N7) = {N6, N7}

# KILL(N8) = {local variables for if-else statement} = EMPTY
# GEN(N8) = EMPTY
In(N8) = Out(N5) U Out(N7) = {N2, N5} U {N6, N7} = { N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N8) = { N2, N5, N6, N7}

# In(N9) = Out(N8) = { N2, N5, N6, N7}
# Out(N9) = { N2, N5, N6, N7, N9}

The node N8 should have killed all variables local for both if-branch and else-branch scopes. Since there is 0 variable local for these scopes, the node kills 0 variables.

docs.title: Static Values Calculation
docs.text:
The RHS of the N1 uses literals only, so the x is static. One assignment to x influences y definition in N1, so both x and y are static at this point. Since N3 and N4 do not modify any state, the static properties of the two variables are transferred. The value of x in the node N5 is influenced by the assignment to this variable in the node N1, so the x is static at this point. Similarly, the x and y hold the static property in nodes N6 and N7 respectively.

Finally, two assignment to x (from nodes N5 and N6) and two assignments to y (from nodes N2 and N7) influences the value of z. Thus, at this point of analysis we cannot determine single values of x and y in the node N9 so we shall treat them as dynamic and mark z variable as dynamic at this point. However, as we shall see bellow, by if statement pruning, as the condition is static, we can calculate and prove that only a single assignment to x and y influence N9 and thus we can prove that z is static.

docs.title: The CFG with a while-statement
docs.text:
Consider the following snippet with a while statement. The N4 node has two successors: N5 and N8. The latter node has only one predecessor N4.

fn foo():
    int x = 0           # N1
    int cnt = 0         # N2
    while cnt < 10:     # N3 is the condition, N4 is the while_statement node
        x = x + cnt     # N5
        cnt += 1        # N6 is equivalent to cnt = cnt + 1
        int y = x       # N7 points back to the N3
    end                 # N8 is the end_while node
   int z = x * cnt      # N9
end

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
The preparation phase outputs the following symbol table:

{
  x: {N1, N5};
  cnt: {N2, N6};
  z: {N9};
  {
    # The scope of the while-statement body
    y: {N7};  
  }
}

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
During the first iteration, the data-flow equation solver is aware only about the Out set of the N2 node, but not of the N7's Out set. Thus, the iteration takes two iterations to converges.

The first iteration of the RDA behaves as follows:

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1, N2}

# KILL(N3) = GEN(N3) = EMPTY since the N3 is the condition node
# As stated abode, the Out(N7) is not evaluated at the moment,
# so use the EMPTY as the appropriate value.
In(N3) = Out(N2) U Out(N7) = {N1, N2} U EMPTY = {N1, N2}
Out(N3) = In(N3) = {N1, N2}

# The while statement node does not kill nor generate any variables,
# thus the KILL(N4) = GEN(N4) = EMPTY
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2}
Out(N4) = In(N3) = {N1, N2}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5}

# N6
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N2, N5}
Out(N6) = {N5, N6}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N5, N6}
Out(N7) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N8 kills variables local for the while-statement scope
# KILL(N8) = {N7}
In(N8) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N8) = {N1, N2}

# N9
In(N9) = Out(N8) = {N1, N2}
Out(N9) = {N1, N2, N9}

The second iteration proceeds as follows:

# The input sets of the N1 and N2 remains the same across iterations,
# so their outputs are unchanged.

# In this iteration, the solver uses the Out(N7) evaluated 
# by the previous iteration.
In(N3) = Out(N2) U Out(N7) = {N1, N2} U {N5, N6, N7} = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N3) = In(N3) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}

# N4
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N4) = In(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5, N6, N7}

# N6
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N6) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N5, N6, N7}
Out(N7) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N8: Consider that KILL(N8) = {N7}
In(N8) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N8) = {N1, N2, N5, N6}

# N9
In(N9) = Out(N8) = {N1, N2, N5, N6}
Out(N9) = {N1, N2, N5, N6, N9}

docs.title: Static Values Calculation
docs.text: Obviously, x and cnt are static in N1 and N2 points. In sets of N3 and N4 indicates the x and cnt are dynamic in these two points. Two assignments to cnt (N2 and N6) and two previous assignment to x (N1 and N5) influence the value of x in N5, meaning the x and cnt remain dynamic in this node. Similarly, the cnt is dynamic in N6. The value of y at N7 program point depends on only assignment to x in N5. Recursively, we visit the node N5 to determine the dynamic nature of x at N5, so we conclude the y is dynamic in N7. From the In set of the node N8, one concludes that both x and cnt are dynamic. At the end, two assignments to x (and cnt respectively) influence the value of z, meaning it is dynamic at this program point.

docs.title: The CFG with a for-statement
docs.text:
Consider the following snippet with a for statement. The N4 node has two successors: N5 and N8. The latter node has only one predecessor N4.

fn foo():
    int x = 1           # N1
    for i = 1:10:        # N2 is the range, N3 is the node that initialize the i,
                        # N4 is the for_statement node
        x = x + i       # N5
        i = 2           # N6
        int y = i + 1   # N7 points back to the N3
    end                 # N8 is the end_for node
    int z = x * 3       # N9
end

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
For the purpose of the for-statements, the TML compiler produces a so-called middle scope nested within the function foo scope, that at the same time encloses the scope of the loop body. Thus, the preparation phase assemble the following symbol table:

{
  x: {N1, N5};
  z: {N9};
  {
    # The for-statement scope that defines the iteration variable.
    i: {N2, N6};
    {
      # The lexical scope belonging to the loop body
      y: {N7};
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
Similarly to the example with the while-statement, the equation solver is not aware of all Out sets corresponding to the N3's dynamic successors, meaning the solution process needs two iterations to stabilize.

The following calculation is done as part of the first iteration.

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2 does not kill, nor generate any variables, so KILL(N3) = GEN(N3) = EMPTY
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1}

# At this moment, the Out(N7) is uknown, so we use the EMPTY as
# an appropriate value.
# N3
In(N3) = Out(N2) U Out(N7) = {N1} U EMPTY = {N1}
Out(N3) = In(N3) = {N1, N2}

# The for_statement not does not produce, nor kills any variables,
# so KILL(N4) = GEN(N4) = EMPTY
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2}
Out(N4) = In(N4) = {N1, N2}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5}

# N6
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N2, N5}
Out(N6) = {N5, N6}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N5, N6}
Out(N7) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N8 kills variables local for the for statement loop 
# (iteration variable + loop body scope)
# KILL(N8) = {N2, N6, N7}
In(N8) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2}
Out(N8) = {N1}

In(N9) = Out(N8) = {N1}
Out(N9) = {N1, N9}

The second iteration proceeds as follows:

# The In sets of N1 and N2remain unchaged, so as theirs Out set.

# Use the Out(N7) calculated in the previous iteration.
In(N3) = Out(N2) U Out(N7) = {N1} U {N5, N6, N7} = {N1, N5, N6, N7}
Out(N3) = {N1, N2, N5, N7}

# N4
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N2, N5, N7}
Out(N4) = In(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N7}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N7}
Out(N5) = {N2, N5, N7}

# N6
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N2, N5, N7}
Out(N6) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N7
In(N7) = Out(N6) = {N5, N6, N7}
Out(N7) = {N5, N6, N7}

# N8: KILL(N8) = {N2, N6, N7}
In(N8) = Out(N4) = {N1, N2, N5, N7}
Out(N8) = {N1, N5}

# N9
In(N9) = Out(N8) = {N1, N5}
Out(N9) = {N1, N5, N9}

docs.title: Static Values Calculation
docs.text:
It is obvious that x is static in both N1 and N2. Since the RHS of the N3 uses literals only, one concludes the i variable is static.

``admonish todo An alternative way is to proclaimias dynamic whenever encountering a node representing initialization of the for-loop iteration variable. Thus, we referi` to be in ITER-VAR-STATE later in the section. 

The `In` set of the node `N4` 
contains two references to the assignments to `x`, meaning `x` is dynamic
at this program point. The `ITER-VAR-STATE` of variable `i` is transferred to the `N4`.

Considering the node `N5`, one notices that two earlier assignments to `x` (`N1` and `N5`)
influence the value of the `x` inside node `N5`, meaning the `x` holds the dynamic property.
Since the `i`'s value is updated inside `N6` with the literal value, the `i` changes its
nature from `ITER-VAR-STATE` to static. 
Furthermore, we conclude that only one assignment to `i` (at the `N6` program
point) influences the `y`, so the `y` share the same property the `i` has in the node `N6`,
which is static.
Trivially, the `x` holds the dynamic property in the node (`N8`).

```admonish question
Do we need to consider the properties of `y`, and `i` that are killed by this node?

Lastly, the RHS of the node N9 references the x whose values depends on two assignments (N1 and N5), meaning the z is dynamic in the N9.

docs.title: Interprocedural analysis (RDA only)
docs.text: The following few examples covers typical cases that should bring more insights about interprocedural dataflow analysis.

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
Consider the following example with pure functions only.

fn foo():
    a = 3      # N1
    bar()      # replaced by the N2
    b = a + 1  # N5
end

fn bar():
    # N2 is the entry node
    a = 4  # N3
    # N4 is the exit node
end

TML compilers copies the CFG of bar at the place of the corresponding call. Nodes are enumerated respecting the previous concatenation of for and bar CFGs. Nodes N2-N4 are actually copies of the original nodes created by the AST threading process. For the brevity, enter and exits nodes of foo are omitted.

The symbol table contains scopes of both foo and bar functions

{
  foo : {
    a: {N1};
    b: {N5};
  }
  bar : {
    a: {N3}; 
  }
}

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
Assuming that the program execution starts from the foo, the RDA behaves as follows:

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1}

# N3
In(N3) = Out(N2) = {N1}
Out(N3) = {N1, N3}

# KILL(N4) = {N3}
In(N4) = Out(N3) = {N1, N3}
Out(N4) = {N1}

# N5
In(N5) = Out(N4) = {N1}
Out(N5) = {N1, N5}

docs.title: Static Value Analysis
docs.text: The N1 assigns the scalar value to a, meaning the a is static at this point. Since the bar does not see nor change the a belonging to the foo, and N5 defines b, the a remains static until foo finishes. The node N3 defines its own a variable, and the RHS of the definitions indicates the static nature of the variable. At the end, the static value of a influences the definition of b inside N5, so the b is static at this point.

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
While executing the preparation phase, the TML compiler uses the information about aliasing explained above to produce the following symbol table:

{
  a: {N1, N7};
  main: {
    b: {N3};
    c: {N11};
    tmp: {N4, N8}
  };
  bar: {
    var: {N6};
  };
}

As one may notice, the temporary variable tmp aliasing bar's second parameter is introduced for display purposes. We still need to decide whether to explicitly introduce the variable, or to hide it somewhere behind the scene.

```admonish todo Should we introduce temporary variables in the symbol table?

igord: We should probably treat them as any other subexpression temporaries. 

### Reaching Definition Analysis (RDA)

The RDA converges in the first iteration shown below:

docs.title: N1
docs.text: In(N1) = EMPTY Out(N2) = {N1}

docs.title: Since the N2 does not receive any arguments, the entry does not introduce
nor kill any nodes.
docs.text: In(N2) = Out(N2) = {N1} Out(N2) = In(N2) = {N1}

docs.title: N4 introduce a temporary variable to hold a constant passes as an argument.
We still need to decide if the introduction of temporary variables shoudl be explicit.
N5 is the entry node of bar, so KILL(N4) = GEN(N4) = EMPTY
N7 - this node is changing the actual argument a belonging to the main
Similarly to the previous node, N8 changes the temporary variable tmp representing constant 3
