N9 is just a return node not killing nor generating anything.
As the exit node, N10 kills variables local to bar function,
N12 kills local variables of main, so KILL(N14) =
docs.text:
In(N12) = Out(N11) = {N3, N7, N8, N11} Out(N12) = {N7} 

### Static Value Calculation

It is obviously that `a`, `b`, and `tmp` are static at `N1-N6`, `N3-N12`, and `N4-N7` 
intervals respectively. 
The node `N6` defines the `var` by using aliases `arg1` and `arg2` to the arguments
`a` and `tmp`, respectively, that are both static, meaning the `var` is static too.
The value of `a` (aliased by `arg1`) depends on its previous value assigned in the node `N1`
and scalar `1`, meaning the `a` remains the same at the point `N7`.
Similarly, one concludes the `tmp` is static at the point `N8`.
All variables hold the determined properties until they are killed by the corresponding
exit nodes.

### Preparation phase

Considering that `arg1` and `arg2` are aliases for `a` and `b` respectively,
the preparation phase outputs the following symbol table.
{ a: {N1, N7, N16}; b: {N2, N8, N17}; main: { res: {N24}; tmp1: {N11, N12}; tmp2: {N20, N21} }; } 
Since a function call behaves as a macro substitution,
a return statement of a callee write to a temporary variable of a caller
allocated at the moment of the actual call (macro substitution).
Thus, we consider that the return statements of the first call to the `sub_abs`
can assign to the `tmp1`. Similarly, the return statements of the second
call can write to the`tmp2`. 
To make this possible, we should store a reference to the temporary variable
of the callers inside the copy of each return statement of a callee.


~~Since the `sub_abs` must return a value, we introduce a pseudo
variable `ret_sub_abs` in the scope of `sub_abs` to ease
the process of analysing static property of the return value.
Additionally, we consider each return statement as the assignment
to the `ret_sub_abs`. If there is more than one definition
of this variable influencing the exit_node of a function,
we conclude that the return value is dynamic.
Furthermore, we assume that this variable is 
assigned to the temporary variables `tmp1` and
`tmp2` of the caller `main` to influence the definition of the `res`.~~

```admonish todo
Check if it is ok to consider a return statement as an assignment 
to the temporary variable of the caller. 
It is a little bit counterintuitive that a store to the 
temporary variable preceeds the exit node.

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
The RDA converges after the first iteration determining the following In/Out:

# N1
In(N1) = EMPTY
Out(N1) = {N1}

# N2
In(N2) = Out(N1) = {N1}
Out(N2) = {N1, N2}

# N3 is the entry node, so KILL(N3) = GEN(N3) = EMPTY
In(N3) = Out(N2) = {N1, N2}
Out(N3) = In(N3) = {N1, N2}

# N4 and N5 are introduced to point out that the `a + b`
# uses values of `a` and `b` before the first call to `sub_abs` is executed.
# These nodes nor KILL, or GEN anything.
Out(N3) = In(N4) = Out(N4) = In(N5) = Out(N5) = {N1, N2}

# As the entry node of the `sub_abs`, the N6 does not KILL or GEN any variable.
In(N6) = Out(N5) = {N1, N2}
Out(N6) = In(N6) = {N1, N2}

# N7 modifies the `arg1` which is the alias for `a`.
In(N7) = Out(N6) = In(N6) = {N1, N2}
Out(N7) = {N2, N7}

# Similarly to the previous node, the N8 modifies `b` though its alias `arg2`
In(N8) = Out(N7) = {N2, N7}
Out(N8) = {N7, N8}

# The condition node N9 and the if_node N10 does not KILL, nor GEN any variables.
Out(N8) = In(N9) = Out(N9) = In(N10) = Out(N10) = {N7, N8}

# The node N11 represents the return statement that updates the `tmp1` belonging
# to the caller.
In(N11) = Out(N10) = {N7, N8}
Out(N11) = {N7, N8, N11}

# Similarly to the N11, the N12 assigns to the `tmp1`.
In(N12) = Out(N10) = {N7, N8}
Out(N12) = {N7, N8, N12}

# Since the N13 is dead, we avoid processing it. 
# If however we still think it is important to process this node,
# then the equations looks like this.
# The join_if node is reponsible to KILL all variables locally
# defined within if-else statement.
# However, the if-else statement of the `sub_abs` does not defines any local
# variables, so the KILL(N13) = EMPTY.
In(N13) = Out(N11) U Out(N12) = {N7, N8, N11} U {N7, N8, N12} = {N7, N8, N11, N12}
Out(N13) = {N7, N8, N11, N12}

# The exit node N14 KILLs variables local for the `sub_abs` function.
# As one may observed, there is no variables locally defined within
# the function, so the KILL(N14) = EMPTY
# Since the `sub_abs` contains two return statements,
# the exit node N14 has the same number of predecessor (N11 and N12)
In(N14) = Out(N11) U (N12) = {N7, N8, N11} U {N7, N8, N12} = {N7, N8, N11, N12}


# Similarly, we process nodes N15-N23
# N15 is the entry node
In(N15) = Out(N14) = {N7, N8, N11, N12}
Out(N15) = In(N15) = {N7, N8, N11, N12}

# N16 updates the `a`
In(N16) = Out(N15) = {N7, N8, N11, N12}
Out(N16) = {N8, N11, N12, N16}

# N17 updates the `b`
In(N17) = Out(N16) = {N8, N11, N12, N16}
Out(N17) = {N11, N12, N16, N17}

# N18 is condition, while N19 is the if_node
Out(N17) = In(N18) = Out(N18) = In(N19) = Out(N19) = {N11, N12, N16, N17}

# N20 stores to the `tmp2`
In(N20) = Out(N19) = {N11, N12, N16, N17}
Out(N20) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20}

# N21 updates the `tmp2`
In(N21) = Out(N19) = {N11, N12, N16, N17}
Out(N21) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N21}

# The N22 is dead, so we ignore it. If we still think analysing this node
# is important, then check the equations of N13.

# N23 is the exit node with two predecessors.
# Since there is no local variables defined within `sub_abs`,
# KILL(23) = EMPTY
In(N23) = Out(N20) U Out(N21} = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20} U {N11, N12, N16, N17, N21}
Out(23) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20, N21}

# N24 introdcues the `res` variable
In(N24) = Out(23) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20, N21}
Out(N24) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20, N21, N24}

# The N25 KILLs local variable of `main`, so KILL(N25) = {N11, N12, N20, N21, N24}
In(N25) = Out(N24) = {N11, N12, N16, N17, N20, N21, N24}
Out(N25) = {N16, N17}

admonish todo Considering the In(N24), one concludes the definitions of `a` and `b` at the points `N16` and `N17`, respectively, influence the value of `res`. However, the subexpression `a + b` depends on the values assigned in `N1` and `N2`, instead. This fact is pointed out, because we should think about the case where this might be problematic.

docs.title: Static value analysis
docs.text:
It is obvious that both a and b variables are static in the interval containing points N1-N6. By using the information about aliases, we noticed that N7 modifies the a. The new value of a at the point N7 depends on the definition of a at the N1 point and scalar, meaning the a remains static at the N7 point. Similarly, the b is static in the N8 node. Furthermore, a and b remain static until the N16 and N17, respectively, that redefines those variables. Nodes N9 and N10 does not change the nature of the variables. The node N11 might assign the return values to the tmp1 belonging to the caller. If during pruning step we notice that this node executes indeed, then the tmp1 os be static. The previous is true for the node N12 too. Since we do not know whether the if or else branch executes until the pruning step starts, we conclude that two definitions of the return value (and tmp1) at N11 and N12, respectively, influence the return value at the node N14. Thus, the value returned by the sub_abs is dynamic at the N14 point.

Similar reasoning applies to nodes N15-N23. Although, N16 and N17 redefines the a and b, respectively, their static nature remains unchanged. Again, two definitions of the return value (at N20 and N21) influence the exit node N23, so the sub_abs returns the value of dynamic nature.

When considering the definition of res inside the N24, we notice the influence of the following definition: - the definition of the temporary variable tmp1 influenced by the assignments: - the potential return value of the first function call to sub_abs inside N11 - the potential return value of the first function call to sub_abs inside N12 - the definition of the temporary variable tmp2 influenced by the assignments: - the potential return value of the second function call to sub_abs inside N20 - the potential return value of the second function call to sub_abs inside N21 - the definition of a at N16. However, the definition of N1 really influence the definition of res. Check the last todo from the previous subsection. - the definition of b at N17. However, the definition of N2 really influence the definition of res. Check the last todo from the previous subsection.

We can conclude that both temporary variable tmp1 and tmp2 are dynamic, making the res to be dynamic too. We conclude static value analysis by stating that nothing changes in the node N25.

docs.title: Guard analysis
docs.text:
Guard analysis is a pass that is used to deremine whether all guarded terminals were used inside guard blocks, either directly, or inside nested blocks. It is performed after effect analysis, because effect analysis is needed to determine which terminals were accessed by functions.

Guard analysis is implemented as a combined preorder and postorder traversal of AST. Information about currently guarded and used variables is stored in a stack of guard contexts. Guard context consists of directly guarded variables (variables guarded by the innermost guard block), inhertied guarded variables (variables guarded by all outer guard blocks) and used variables (variables used in this scope and all nested scopes).

Guard analysis is performed using following steps: 1. In preorder visit of guard blocks, a new guard context is added to the stack. Variables directly guarded in this context using exists, feedthrough and not feedthrough are added to directly guarded variables. If parent context exists, all direct and inherited guarded variables are added to inherited guarded variables of new context. 2. All child statements are traversed, and variables accessed directly in code are added to the context list of used variables. Variables used by functions are marked as used if they originate from blocks reachable blocks (i.e. not nested inside an opposite guard block). 3. In postorder visit of guard blocks, their guard context gets removed from the stack. If a parent context exists, it inherits all used variables inside the child context. This is necessary for properly detecting usage of guarded variables in nested blocks. A check is performed if all directly guarded variables (that are not inherited guarded, i.e. that are not guarded both in this and outer blocks) are in used list. Error reporting is postponed for inherited guarded variables until their direct guarded context is reached. Note that if not used in this child context, inherited guarded variables can be used inside another child context, so error reporing must always correspond to their direct guard block.

docs.title: Interprocedural analysis
docs.text:
Interprocedural analysis is a mechanism for performing optimization (or other analysis) across function and compilation unit boundaries.

In TML, we distinguish two different function types: builtin and custom- defined functions. The definitions of the builtin functions are integrated in the TML compiler. Their source code cannot be investigated nor changed by the user. One can learn more about this type of function in the official TML documentation. When calling a function of this type, the user observes the same behavior as in other familiar languages. Particularly, if the function call can be statically resolved, the TML compiler substitutes the call with the return value. Otherwise, it generates the call to the C counterpart that usually belongs to the math.h module. Since all builtin functions are pure with no side effects, the TML compiler ignores them during Reachable Definitions Analysis.

On the other hand, custom-defined user functions can have side effects. While TML does inline function calls, ordinary function call semantics are respected. Parameters are passed by value, so only global state changes can be observed as side effects. Custom functions cannot change values from the caller scope.

After building a unique CFG describing the whole program, intraprocedural analysis is applied. Function CFGs are inlined in places of function calls to enable context-aware interprocedural analysis.

```admonish note TML does not statically evaluate values yet. 

```admonish note
In the current version, the interprocedural analysis will operate on a single
compilation unit (TML file). In future versions, we shall work on optimization
across TML files (e.g., at the level of the whole model).

docs.title: Io
docs.text: IO

docs.title: IO variable representation
docs.text: TML represents IO interfaces to users via IO variables (described in detail in user documentation). IO variables are added to the symbol table, like user and model scope variables. Every symbol table entry is assigned a name, type and object. Symbol name and type are used for ordinary semantic analysis, applied to all expressions in TML. The object denotes special IO-related attributes needed in code generation. While name and type enable abstract interface over configurable IO, the object enables specialized logic code generation for a concrete instance of model and component configuration.

docs.title: IO variable types
docs.text:
Currently, TML only supports IO variables that represent communication with FPGA solver. Two supported ways of communicating with FPGA solver are basic IO and DMA. The TML compiler decides concrete IO implementation depending on user settings, IO variable declaration and device capability. Declaring a DMA variable does not mean that the compiler will enforce DMA mechanism. DMA must be enabled in model settings and supported by the device in order to be used. If enabled and supported, all model variables declared as DMA variables will use DMA, otherwise basic IO is used as a fallback.

For both FPGA IO variable types, the end address of the destination IO device must be manually specified. TML allows only expressions that can be statically evaluated to be used for addresses to prevent any possible ambiguities about resolution order. IO variable addresses are resolved when compiling components. If addresses need to be parameterized using hardware or model configuration, address values must be calculated inside component handlers and passed via model scope.

docs.title: Basic IO
docs.text: The main interface used for basic IO by TML are atomic HIL_InInt32/HIL_OutInt32 and HIL_InFloat/HIL_OutFloat functions. Atomic functions work on scalar types. Scalar writes consist of calling IO functions with value and address as parameters. Vector writes consist of calling a sequence of atomic operations, with the address being incremented by one for every next element. If broadcasting is applied, the same scalar value is written on all addresses belonging to vector IO variable address range.

docs.title: DMA
docs.text: HIL devices support scatter-gather DMA approach. This means that destination IO devices are viewed as non-contiguous (i.e. scattered) address ranges, while structures for reading and writing values from and to IO devices are contiguous address ranges (hence IO gathers values on read and scatters them on write). DMA has 12 read channels and 12 write channels. Every channel is a separate contiguous address space visible to TML. End device destination addresses are the same as in basic IO and the same manual mechanism is used for resolving them. Internal DMA structure and channel addresses are automatically resolved. Upon resolving internal DMA addresses, the DMA controller is configured to map internal and external addresses.

docs.title: DMA execution cycle
docs.text:
Before explaining how code for DMA is generated, we must first understand how DMA works and what are the main rules for DMA controller configuration and DMA channel allocation. Lower ID CPU has higher priority than higher ID CPU (i.e. CPU0 has higher priority than CPU1). Faster execution rate has higher priority than a slower one. When allocating channels, used CPUs are first sorted, followed by sorting ERs per CPU. Read and write DMA channels are allocated separately.

Detailed DMA execution steps are:

  1. During model initialization, for every DMA channel, DMA controller must be configured with external space address and size and matching internal address
  2. Before running simulation cycle, DMA read synchronize sequence must be performed to move all current input values from external address spaces to internal ones
  3. After running simulation cycle, DMA write synchronize sequence must be performed to move all current output values from internal address spaces to external ones

docs.title: DMA code generation
docs.text:
Direct association of DMA variables and channels would make compilation significantly more complicated. DMA channels cannot be allocated before all TML components are analyzed, as the number of DMA variables is not known before type analysis (preceded by parsing and macro expansion). Such kind of interdependency presents a common problem in compilers: an example would be how linkers are used to combine separate portions of code. Thus, TML compiler adopts context-free approach. Every DMA variable has unique name, and channels are referenced by their deterministic names that consist of IO direction, CPU ID and execution rate. TML compiler outputs context-free DMA variable metadata for every component, and only after all components were analyzed, the compiler metadata is used for allocating DMA channels and DMA controller configuration sequences.

Detailed steps performed on TML compiler side are:

  1. For every component, TML analyzes whether IO variable will be generated using basic IO or DMA (depending on model configuration, hardware configuration and IO variable declaration)
  2. For every DMA variable in component, TML compiler stores metadata about size, address and target CPU and ER
  3. IO operations are basic variable reads and writes, with only variable codegen name having DMA struct in prefix
  4. On component level, TML generates code that assumes constant channel name
  5. After all components were compiled, metadata is gathered and concrete DMA channels are assigned on model level. DMA channel metadata is exported for THCC

Detailed steps performed on THCC side are:

  1. DMA channel and variable metadata is read
  2. IO channel structures are generated and global order of IO variables in internal address space is selected
  3. IO structs are assigned to address spaces with deterministic names that are allocated in linker script (using symbolic names wherever possible is good practice from code readability standpoint)
  4. DMA controller configuration sequences are generated for every DMA channel, manual external and automatic internal addresses are mapped
  5. DMA synchronization sequences are generated for every DMA channel
  6. Address spaces are generated in the linker script starting from lower to higher channel numbers. Channel addresses are cache-line size aligned as that is required by ARM cache-coherent communication interface

docs.title: Macro system
docs.text:
Three main approaches to macro systems exist:

  1. Pure source code manipulation
  2. This approach is taken by C
  3. Macros enable interpolating and pruning text
  4. If an error in generated code exists, it is hard to find as the report is unreadable
  5. This approach was heavily criticized even during the 1960s
  6. Turing-complete reflective AST manipulation
  7. Introduced by Lisp
  8. Modern languages taking this approach are Julia, Scala, Nim
  9. AST constructs can be represented as data structures that are arbitrarily constructed and manipulated
  10. Program can choose to construct new program parts and run (evaluate) them
  11. Quite complicated to implement and users are required to understand compilers in-depth
  12. AST level preprocessor
  13. Hybrid approach that combines previous two in a simplified static manner
  14. Rust takes this approach
  15. Special syntax is needed to denote, define and include macros
  16. Instead of emitting strings, AST nodes are created
  17. Reported errors are understandable and can be traced
  18. Macros are analyzed before expansion which additionally improves error reporting

TML uses AST level preprocessor. This enables TML programs to write themselves using parametrization from model scope, but still enables proper syntax and semantic analysis. Macro code can contain only valid TML syntax and readable errors are reported before macro expansion. After expansion, origin of new AST nodes can be linked with source code lines before expansion, alongside macro expansion state (counter variables) for readable error reporting.

docs.title: Macro resolver
docs.text:
Macro resolution in TML consists of two basic steps:

  1. Macro analysis: original AST is analyzed, errors are reported if macro variables overlap. Additional metadata needed for later expansion is generated
  2. Macro expansion: expressions of macro nodes are statically evaluated, portions of code are either duplicated or removed, per specific macro rules

docs.title: Macro analysis
docs.text:
Macro analysis performs following steps:

  1. Original (non-expanded) AST is traversed
  2. Stacks of current macro for nodes and corresponding expansion variable names are created and maintained by pushing in preorder traversal and popping in postorder traversal
  3. Upon encountering statements that generate new macro variables or ordinary user variables, current expansion stack is analyzed and errors are reported on name clash
  4. Errors are reported if macros present in global scope contain statements not allowed in global scope (all statements besides variable declarations)
  5. Upon encountering macro variable references, parent macro for node is read from the stack, and new child reference is added (needed later for easier macro expansion)

docs.title: Macro expansion
docs.text:
1. Stack of expansion variables is created and maintained. In contrast to the stack used in macro analysis, values are also stored. Values of variables correspond to macro expansion iteration based on ranges in parent macro for
  2. Upon encountering macro if, type analysis is performed over expression. If possible, expression is statically evaluated. Otherwise, error is reported.
  3. All macro if conditions are analyzed and evaluated and all errors are reported. If no errors are reported, first true branch is selected (if exists)
  4. Upon encountering macro for, range expression is analyzed and evaluated. Errors are reported if needed. Otherwise, expansion is performed
  5. Inner statement block is copied as many times as needed. Deepcopy preserves child links between expansion state and variable references. Copied child list enables simple annotation of variable nodes with corresponding values.

docs.title: Additional remarks
docs.text:
Macro expansion works only over child nodes of macro statement blocks. While later analyses treat both original and expanded AST nodes equally, resulting AST is not the same. There were two reasons behind this decision:

  1. Expanding AST to look like it originates from user-written source code is an order of magnitude more complicated, as it requires complex child-parent double-link manipulation
  2. There is no added benefit or downside in later analyses and code generation using either approach
  3. When testing and debugging macro expansion, easy origin tracking is desirable

docs.title: Optional type
docs.text:
TML supports optionally present variables. Terminals can be optionally present depending on the model configuration. User defined variables can be optionally present only if their type is derived from an optionally present terminal.

TML makes a distinction between optionally present variables and values. All variables, whether optionally present or not, must be defined in symbol table. When accessing optionally present variables, their presence can be proved only using exists guard block.

On the other hand, values in expressions can be optionally present only if the variable was inline guarded. Functions can be called in different contexts, and treating unguarded optional variables differently in separate guard contexts would be both confusing and hard to implement. Because of this, all unguarded accesses assume that a variable is guarded using a guard block, or raise an error otherwise.

docs.title: Optional type inference
docs.text:
TMLOptional is a wrapper type that contains base type that can be either a tensor, a scalar or TML_MISSING. TML_ERROR is never wrapped inside TMLOptional. TMLOptional type is created only when variables are guarded using inline guard operator ?.

In binary expressions, if both types are TMLOptional, base type is unwrapped and new base type is inferred using regular type inference. Outer type remains optional, as both subexpressions can optionally be present.

If only one type is TMLOptional, and the other is not, resulting type will not be optional, as expression value is defined in all possible cases. If optional value is missing, inferred type is equal to the present type. If optional value is present, it's base type is unwrapped and type is inferred using regular type inference.

docs.title: Optional type assignability
docs.text: TMLOptional is not assignable to any type, regardless of which type is wrapped inside. To use TMLOptional in assignments, final elvis expression is needed. Elvis expression always returns a non-optional type, either unwrapped from left branch, or directly from right branch.

docs.title: Preparation phase
docs.text:
The following section assumes the symbol table contains sets of definitions only. The preparation phase is responsible to determine places where every variable is modified. The following pseudo-code provides more information about how the preparation phase works starting from the preparation_phase.

Is one iteration for preparation phase elegant enough

When encountering a call to an unprocessed function, we could try to process it first before continuing processing the caller. An alternative would be to process function definitions during the first iteration, and introducing a second iteration responsible to process function calls only.

ST = symbol_table()

def process_scope(AST_nodes):
  scope = ST.create_scope(ST)
  # processing AST nodes corresponding the scope
  for node in AST_nodes.iterate()
    if node.is_var_def():
      process_var_def()
    else if node.is_selection():
      process_selection()
    else if node.is_for_loop():
      process_for()
    else if node.is_while_loop():
      process_while()
    else if node.is_function_call():
      # Could be shifted to the second iteration
      process_function_call()

  ST.exit_scope()


# Create an entry in the current scope for the `var` if the entry
# does not already exist. One can consider an entry as the 
# Python set containing ids of nodes that (re)defines variables.
# TODO: Storing pseudo-generated ids seems as a more bullet-proof
# solution than storing plain references,
# since a node can be copied while resolving
# function calls
# Generating ids could take place while doing AST walk of preparation phase. 
def ST.find_entry(var):
  # ST might have an internal state aware of current scope we are processing.
  if var in ST:
    # This is true if ST contains the var in any of the active scopes
    # (the scope currently being processed and all enclosing ancestors)
    return ST[var]
  else:
    return ST.create_entry(var)


# Process statements of a function definition as a separate scope
def process_func_def(func_def_node):
  # Note, this function scope should not be nested withing the caller's scope.
  # Thus, we explicitly position below the global scope.
  caller_scope = position_below_global_scope()
  process_scope(func_def_node)
  # Return to the caller scope and continue processing it.
  ST.set_scope(caller)

# Processing an AST node matching declarations and assignments
def process_var_def(definition_node):
  variable = definition_node.LHS
  entry = ST.find_entry(variable)
  # Add a new defintion in the set of defitions
  entry.add_def(definition.id)


# When processing a selection statement, we need to process
# all if-else-if-else branches and create corresponding
# scope for each of them.
def process_selection(selection_node):
  for branch in selection_branches():
    process_scopes(branch.statements)


# Before processing body as a separate scope,
# introduce a scope containing iteration variable only
def process_for_loop(for_node):
  # introduce a scope containing iteration variable only
  scope = ST.create_scope()
  # Find the iteration variable
  iter_var = for_node.iteration_variable
  entry = ST.find_entry(iter_var)    
  entry.add_def()
  # Process body as a separate scope
  process_scopes(for_node.body.statements)
  # Exit the scope containing the iteration variable
  ST.exit_scope()


# Processing statements of a while node as a separate scope.
def process_while_loop(while_node):
  process_scopes(while_node.statements)


def process_function_call(func_call_node):
  func_id = func_call_node.id
  # find the node representing the function definition
  func_def = AST.find_func_def()
  if not func_def.is_processed():
    # Process the function definition if not processed already.
    process_function(func_def)

  mappings = {}
  # Iterate over (arg, param) pairs
  for (arg, param) in func_node.args, func_def.params:
    if arg.is_LHS():                        # in the caller scope
      arg_entry = ST.find_entry(arg)
      param_entry = ST.find_entry(param)
      # Extend the set of arg with all definitions of the param,
      # thus introducing side effects of the call.
      # The `extend` method is actually union.
      arg_entry.extend(param_entry)
    else if arg.is_function_call():
      process_function_call()

    # Create arg:param mappings
    # For convenience keep reference to the symbol table entries
    # corresponding arguments and parameters. 
    # If the arg is not LHS object (e.g. expression or constant), arg_entry
    # is None.
    mappings.add(arg, param, arg_entry, param_entry) 

def preparation_phase():
  global_scope = ST.create_scope()
  for node in AST.iterate_nodes:
    if node.is_var_def():
      process_var_def()
    else is node.is_func_def()
      process_func_def()
  ST.exit_scope()

Consider the following example and the enumeration of nodes. For brevity, enter and exits nodes are omitted.

a = 5                 # N1

fn main():
  foo(a)              # N2
  foo(a)              # N3
end


fn foo(arg):
  arg += 1            # N4
  b = 3               # N5
  bar(arg, b)         # N6
end


fn bar(arg1, arg2):
  arg1 += 1           # N7
  arg2 *= 2           # N8
end

Preparation phase starts by processing the global scope, determining the a has been changed by the node N1. Next, we start processing the main that calls foo inside node N2. Since the foo is not present in the symbol table, the process moves to reason about that function. We determine the foo's scope with all variables modified by the foo (arg1, and b respectively). We then encounter on the node N6 representing the function call to the unprocessed function bar, so we switch to processing it. As one may notice after observing the corresponding scope, the bar modifies the arg1 and arg2, respectively.

After finishing with bar, we continue processing the call to bar inside the node N6. As we may notice, arg is actually changed in the nodes changing arg1 parameter. Thus, we add elements of sets corresponding to arg1 ({N7}) to the set corresponding the arg (currently {N4} only) by applying union between these two sets (the result set {N4, N7}). Similarly, b is modified in the nodes modifying arg2.

The next step is to finish processing the call to foo at the N2 node. As one may notice, the nodes changing arg also changing the a, thus we use the elements of the set corresponding to arg and appends (union) to the a's set.

Finally, we process the second call to foo in the node N3. Since the foo has already been processed, it remains to check the redefinitions of a through param arg. Thus, we apply the union on the set corresponding the a and arg. As we may notice, there is not any changes in the result set of a, concluding the preparation phase which outputs the table shown below.

{
  a: {N1, N4, N7};
  main: {};
  foo: {
    arg: {N4, N7},
    b: {N5, N8}
  }
  bar: {
    arg1: {N7},
    arg2: {N8}
  }
}

Todo

I suppose we do not need to create distiction between original nodes of the function and copies of nodes that replace the call to the function.

docs.title: Building CFG Matching The Program Execution
docs.text:
Before analysing the program behavior, we should determine the CFG matching the program execution. For this purpose, we use a knowledge about how the component is executed on HIL. The first step is to determine (execute/interpret) global definitions of the component. Next, the init function of the component is called to properly set up the component. Afterwards, output and update functions are interchangeably called for the duration of the simulation. From the perspective of the TML compiler, currently able to reason about only one component, the execution of the component is abstracted in the snippet below:

global_scope_definitions

init()

while true
  output()
  update()

To build the graph manifested in the previous snippet, the TML compiler connects the last declaration of the global scope with the entry node of the init function's CFG. Afterwards, the compiler generates a while loop with true condition, whose body starts with the entry node belonging to the output function. To ensure the update function is executed after the output, we connect the output's exit node to the update's entry node. We choose to inline functions init, output, and updated instead to introduce calls to them that will be replaced during the process described below. The implementation of connecting global scope with functions init, output, and update, respectively, is present inside the method inter_function_ast_threading of the ASTThreading class.

Since calls to custom-defined functions (including init, output, and update) behave as macro substitutions, the TML compiler traverses the previously determined CFG and replaces each call with the copy of called function's CFG. Consider the following pseudo-code starting from the inline_function_calls.

The callstack represents the stack of function calls matching the one present during single-threaded execution of a program.

# NOTE: While doing CFG traversal, we should mark visited nodes to avoid cycling.
# The following code is not burden with marking statements like:
# `node.is_visited = true`
# They reside explicitly in places where that takes car of of jumps/cycles. 

CALL_STACK = init_stack()

# The start node of CFG is the first declaration of global scope if any.
# Otherwise, the first node is the `init` funciton.

def inline_function_calls(CFG):
  for node in CFG.nodes.iterate():
    if node.is_call_to_custom_function():
      # If the node represents a call to the custom-defined function,
      # inline the call by copying the CFG of the function
      inline_call(node)
    else:
      # Otherwise, do nothing for the node.


def inline_func_call(function_call_node):
  # add function call to the stack
  push_call(function_call_node)

  # The function call has exactly one predecessor
  pred = call_node.predecessor

  # The function call has exactly one successor node.
  succ = fc.successor

  # Copy the CFG of the function between the function call's predecessor (`pred`)
  # and (`next`)  
  copy_exit_node = copy_path(pred, fc.entry)

  # connect the copy of the exit_node with the successor of the function call
  connect_nodes(copy_exit_node, succ)

  # pop the function call from the stack 
  pop_call()


# This funciton copies the path starting with the `start_node` and 
# place it immediately after the `pred` node.
# The path usually ends with a node taking one of the foollowing types:
# function exit node, join_if node, for_node, while_node, end_while, and end_for node)
def copy_path(pred, pred2?, start_node):
  curr_node = start_node
  while curr and not cur.is_path_end():         # While node is not the end of a path, process it in the following way
    if curr_node.is_func_call():
      inline_func_call(pred, pred2, curr_node)
    else if curr_node.is_selection(curr_node)
      pred, curr = copy_selection(pred, pred2, curr_node)
    else if curr_node.can_be_jumped():
      # Check if the node has already been visited. If so, stop cycling.
      if curr_node.is_visited:
        reuturn pred
      # The current node belongs to this path and once more that intersects it.
      # One can consider this node as the destination of the jump belonging to the loop. 
      pred, curr, already_processed = copy_jump(pred, pred2, curr_node)
    else:
      # This function copies the `curr`, connects the copy to the `pred`
      # and returns the copy and `curr`'s sucessor as new (pred, curr_node) pair.
      pred, curr = copy_regular_node(pred, curr_node)

  if curr:
    if curr.is_function_exit():
      # copy the function exit node that is considered as the end of path
      curr_copy = COPY_NODE(curr)
      return curr_copy
    else if curr.is_join_if_node():
      # Returning a node preceeding the join node.
      # Check `copy_selection` for more information
      return pred
    else if curr.is_loop_node():
      # The `copy_jump` is reponsible to process this node
      return curr
  else
    return pred


def copy_selection(pred, if_node)
  # The condition preceeding the `if_node` has been already copied
  # as part of the path (done by the `copy_path` function).
  copy_if_node = COPY_NODE(pred, if_node)

  # Nodes at the beginning of the if-branch and else-branch, respectively
  left_succ = if_node.successors[0]
  right_succ = if_node.successors[1]

  # Copy paths matching if and else branches.
  # Note that COPY_PATH returns a node preceeding the join_if_node
  before_join_left = copy_path(copy_if_node, left_succ)
  before_join_right = copy_path(copy_if_node, right_succ)

  # copy the end node and connect with both last nodes of 
  # if and else branches, respectively
  join_if_node = before_join_left.sucessor
  copy_join_if_node = COPY_NODE(join_if_node)
  connect_nodes(before_join_left, copy_join_if_node)
  connect_nodes(before_join_right, copy_join_if_node)

  return copy_join_if_node, join_if_node.successor


def copy_jump(pred, jump_node):
  # Check if the `jump_node` has already been visited.
  if jump_node.is_visited:
    return pred, jump_node

  jump_node.is_visited = true

  # The `pred` comes from the path preceeding the loop.
  # The `jump_node` represents either the first node of 
  # while loop condition, or a node initilizing iteration variable
  # of a for loop.

  # copy the jump node and connect it with predecessor
  copy_jump_node = COPY_NODE(jump_node)
  connect_nodes(pred, copy_jump_node)
  succ = jump_node.successor

  # copy the path (contains condition or a initialization of the iteration variable)
  # until reaching the for_node or a while_node (referred to as loop_node)
  loop_node = copy_path(succ)
  copy_loop_node = COPY_NODE(loop_node)

  # the `loop_node` has two successor, the first successor is at the beginning of the body,
  # while the second represents the end_loop node.
  start_body_node = loop_node.successors[0]
  end_loop = loop_node.successors[1]

  # Calling `copy_path` results in reaching the jump_node again.
  # Since it has already been processed, do not process it twice.
  # Instead, return the last node of the body that jumps to (preceeds) the `jump_node`,
  copy_last_body_node = copy_path(start_body_node, start_body_node)

  # connect the copy_last_body_node with copy_jump_node
  connect_nodes(copy_last_body_node, copy_jump_node)

  copy_end_loop = COPY_NODE(end_loop)
  # connect the `loop_node` with the `copy_end_loop`
  connect_nodes(copy_loop_node, copy_end_loop)

  return copy_end_node, end_node.successor


def copy_regular_node(pred, node):
   # actual copying
   copy_node = COPY_NODE(node)

   connect_nodes(pred, copy_node)

   # Substitute params with args 
   substitute_params_with_args(node)

   return copy_node


# TODO: Need to find a good way to do this kind of replacement
# Is it enough just to replace references to entries matching
# params to entries matching args?
def substitute_params_with_args(node):
  params_args_mapping = STACK.top.mapping
  if LHS.is_param():
    LHS.replace_params_with_args()
  else:
    RHS.replace_params_with_args()


def COPY_NODE(node):
  # DEEP copy of a node..

docs.title: Reaching Definition Analysis
docs.text:
Reaching definition analysis represents the algorithm responsible to determine In/Out sets for every node in the CFG calculated by previous step (check the previous section). The algorithm iteratively solves equations described in Data-Flow Equations section until fixpoint is reached. The following snippet shows the pseudo-code that elaborates about the algorithm

def RDA(CFG):
    converged = false
    while not converged:
        # Assume the algorithm converges in this iteration.
        converged = true
        for node in CFG.nodes.iterator():
            unchanged = process_node(node)
            # If all nodes remain unchanged, the outer while loop breaks.
            converged = converged AND unchanged


# Solve equations for the `node`
# The return value indicates 
def process_node(node):
    old_in = node.In
    new_in = calc_new_in(node)

    # If the In set remains unchanged, the Out will remain unchanged too
    if old_in == new_in:
        return true

    # store new In set
    node.In = new_in

    kill = node.find_kill_set()
    gen = node.find_gen_set()

    # Calculate the out set of the node
    node.Out = (node.In \ kill) U gen

    return false


def node.find_kill_set(node):
    if node.is_var_def():
        # The node representing a variable definition (declaration or assignment)
        # has a reference to the corresponding symbol table entry representing
        # the KILL set.
        return node.symbol_table_entry
    # ...
    # For the majority of other types of nodes the KILL set is EMPTY.
    # TODO: think about this during the implementation phase.

def node.find_gen_set(node):
    if node.is_var_def():
        # GEN set for the variable definition is the set containing node's id.
        return {node.id}
    # ...
    # For the majority of other types of nodes the KILL set is EMPTY.
    # TODO: think about this during the implementation phase.


# Calculate In set of the `node` by applying union on Out sets of `node`'s
# dynamic predecessors.
def calc_new_in(node):
    In = set()
    for pred in node.predecessors:
        In = In U pred.Out

docs.title: Static Value Calculation
docs.text:
The Static Value Calculation (SVC) process is responsible to determine whether variables used by a node N are static, dynamic, or undefined, respectively, in this node. The sections Static Values Calculation provides the overview about this process. The following snippet provides an idea how this might be implemented in the TML compiler.

def static_value_calculation(CFG):
    for node in CFG.nodes.iterator():
        process_node(node)

def process_node(node):
    if node.is_var_def():
        process_var_def():
    # ...
    # Processing other nodes should be similar.
    # TODO: discuss more about this while implementing SVC


# We need to determine the property of the RHS to be able
# to say if the LHS is static, dynamic, or undefined. 
def process_var_def(node):
    propety = STATIC
    for subexpr_node in node.RHS.expression.subexpressions_nodes():
        subexpr_prop = expr_property(subexpr_node, node.In)
        if subexpr_prop == DYNAMIC:
            property = DYNAMIC
        else if subexpr_prop == UNDEFINED:
            RAISE AN ERROR

    node.LHS.property = propety

# The function checks whether the `expr_node` being part of the expressions
# is static, dynamic, or undefined.
# The `influence_set` represents a set of definition influencing the `expr_node.
def expr_property(expr_node, influence_set):
    if expr_node.is_var():
        # The node is variable that reference the corresponding symbol table entry.
        entry_set = expr_node.symbol_table_entry
        # We apply the intersection of `entry_set` and the `influence_set`.
        intersection = entry_set INTERSECTION influence_set
        # If the `intersection` has no elements, the variable is undefined.
        # If the `intersetion has exactly one element, then variable is static at this point.
        # Otherwise, the variable is dynamic.
        if intersection.len == 0:
            return UNDEFINED
        else if intersection.len == 1:
            return STATIC
        else:
            return DYNAMIC.

    # For other types of subexpression it should be realavely straightforward
    # to determine their static property.
    # TODO: Think more about this while implementing SVC

docs.title: Schematic Editor integration
docs.text:
Note

All TML related changes in Schematic Editor can be found on tml-develop branch.

TML compiler is called like any other compiler in SE, via utilizing the subprocess module.

docs.title: Model preparation
docs.text: Before the TML compiler is called, some small preparations are being made during creation of the reduced model. Since we are calling TML just like any other external compiler, we need to store the results (generated C code) in a directory that can be later on utilized by the code generator. In our case, we are creating a temp directory in convert_core_model() and use it for the current compilation.

docs.title: Calling the TML compiler
docs.text: The function that communicates with the TML compiler is called call_tml and can be found here: t_sw/thcc/typhoon/tools/compiler/code_generation/generator/compile.py::371. The call_tml() calls TML compiler and stores the result (compiled C code) into the previously mentioned temp directory.

docs.title: The compile command
docs.text:
TML compiler comes with a compile command that compiles all the components from the model that have TML implementation. The compile command comes with the following arguments:

  • model_file_path - a path to the serialized Schematic model
  • components_impl_path - a dir that contains component implementations in TML
  • --output-dir (optional) - a dir where the generated C code will be stored

Once the command is executed, the compiler will first create an object representation of the model. After that, all TML implementations from the dir defined by components_impl_path are loaded. Finally, the compiler will iterate through the model and compile every components that has a TML implementation.

If the optional --output-dir argument is omitted, the compiler will not generate any files, but it will still perform a complete analysis and even call the code generator. However, if the argument is provided, for each component, the following files will be generated:

  • <component_fqn>.var - defines all variables that will be used in the C code,
  • <component_fqn>.init_fnc - generated C code for the init_fnc,
  • <component_fqn>.output_fnc - generated C code for the output_fnc,
  • <component_fqn>.update_fnc - generated C code for the update_fnc,
  • <component_fqn>.fnc - generated C code for all user-defined functions,
  • <component_fqn>.proto - defines prototypes for all functions from TML file, and
  • <component_fqn>.error - contains all errors and warnings for the corresponding component.

``admonish note For all components in the model with a TML implementation corresponding C code is generated duringcall_tml()`, before the C code generator in SE is called. 

### stout, stderr, status code

The TML compiler will print all information during the compilation into the console.
Status message (i.e. compilation started/finished) will be printed to the `sys.stdout`.
Warnings and errors are printed to the `sys.stderr`.

If the compilation is completed successfully, the compiler will return `0` as the status code.
If the compilation of any of the components fails, the compiler will return `-1` as the
status code.

Schematic editor checks the status code in `call_tml()` function. If the TML compiler
doesn't return `0`, the compilation of the whole model will be stopped.

## Integration with SE C code generator

When preparing data for templates, C code generator detects whether the code
for the corresponding component is already generated by TML. If it is, the
code will be included into the template data (check `prepare_component_data()`
in `generator/base.py`).

```admonish note
Data is sent into templates as a dict. Meaning, generated code is stored in the
dict with `tml_` prefix (e.g. init function will be stored as `tml_init_fnc`).

In templates, for each function we simply check if the corresponding key is defined in the template data. For example,

{# from common_int2_handler_function.template #}
{% for c in components_per_exec_rate[i] -%}
{% if "tml_output_fnc" in c %}
    {{c.tml_output_fnc}}
{% else %}
    {# code that calls template instead #}
{% endfor %}

To support user-defined functions, common_HIL_functions.template is updated with the following:

// TML functions

{% for c in component_list -%}
{% if "tml_fnc" in c%}
{{c.tml_fnc}}
{% endif %}
{% endfor %}

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
During the implementation of TML, there were several workaround (hacks) in SE side that should be addressed properly.

Next sections contain examples where we needed to introduce hacks to help TML, or bugs that often occurred.

``admonish note All issues that require changes in the Schematic Editor are labeled withschematic-rework` label. 

### Dynamic terminals ([Trac 6887](https://trac.typhoon-hil.com/ticket/6887))

We use `dynamic` keyword to define whether a terminal is dynamic or not. However, there is no way to tell if a terminal is optional (either exists or not), or we can have multiple instances of the terminal (i.e. input terminals for Sum, Product,...).

A much more informative way would be to use cardinal numbers. A cardinality of a terminal would be defined as a pair of lower and upper bounds. In our case, we will most often see the following cardinalities defined:

- (0, 1) - optional terminal. It can either not exist, or maximum one instance can be instantiated,
- (0, *) - dynamic terminal. No restrictions in min and max number of instances,
- (1, *) - dynamic terminal where at least one terminal must be instantiated
- (1, N) - dynamic terminal where a number of terminals must be between 1 and N.


For example, optional terminals can be defined like this:
in { cardinality=(0, 1) kind = sp label = "" position = top center direction = in sp_type { supported = int, real default = real } } 
A biggest advantage of this approach is that the validation can be integrated directly into Schematic Editor, and manual code for such validations can be removed.

In addition, this feature is needed for TML project to implement guards properly.

```admonish note
At the moment, this is handled manually in `reduced_sch_model/export.py`, where
`CARDINAL_NUM_PER_CMP_TERM` dict defines a cardinal number for each dynamic terminal.

docs.title: Namespace variables (Trac 6886)
docs.text:
Currently, namespace variables are defined inside pre_compile or post_resolve handlers, in an arbitrary way.

Problem that often occurs is that a namespace variable is declared within if-else clause, which can cause problems during code generation. The latest example of such problem was with discrete_state_space.cmp where a template tried to access variable that is never defined:

A_all_zeros, B_all_zeros, C_all_zeros, D_all_zeros, dss_mat_csr = convert_dense_dss_to_csr(A, B, C, D)
A_mat, B_mat, C_mat, D_mat = dss_mat_csr
if A_all_zeros is False:
    A_values, A_indices, A_indptr, A_values_list, A_indices_list, A_indptr_list, a_values_rows, a_indices_rows, a_indptr_rows, a_rows = A_mat
if B_all_zeros is False:
    B_values, B_indices, B_indptr, B_values_list, B_indices_list, B_indptr_list, b_columns, b_values_rows, b_indices_rows, b_indptr_rows, b_rows = B_mat
if C_all_zeros is False:
    C_values, C_indices, C_indptr, C_values_list, C_indices_list, C_indptr_list, c_values_rows, c_indices_rows, c_indptr_rows, c_rows = C_mat
if D_all_zeros is False:
    D_values, D_indices, D_indptr, D_values_list, D_indices_list, D_indptr_list, d_values_rows, d_indices_rows, d_indptr_rows, d_rows = D_mat

As we can see, there are cases where some of the variables are defined only if certain condition is met. This, in conjunction with pretty complicated templates, makes debugging extra hard.

In addition, with the development of TML, it would be really beneficial to have as complete component description as possible because it will provide more precise type checking and more concise C code.

One way to provide better definition of namespace variables would be to expand .cmp file with a section similar to properties and terminals. For example:

namespace_vars {
   A_values {
     type = real[],
     default_value = [1, 2, 3]  // optional, if omitted default_value will be None
   }

}

The migration to more formal way of defining namespace variables can be implemented gradually. At the beginning, we can allow both formal and informal namespace variable definition, then slowly deprecate the informal way.

Note

We need to pay extra attention to user libraries, and we will probably need to add option for defining namespace variables in the Mask Editor.

docs.title: Schematic model (de)serialization
docs.text:
The modules for creating a reduced schematic model and (de)serialization are tightly coupled with the Schematic core (check export.py and serialize.py for example).

In order to keep TML as separate as possible from THCC toolchain, a simplifier version of reduced model is implemented and can be found in tml/thcc module.

However, a major drawback of this approach is that there is always a possibility of getting out of sync with the modules in Schematic Editor.

docs.title: Static values calculation
docs.text:
For each program point and each variable usage we shall look at the set of its reachable definitions calculated by DFA.

  • If there is zero reachable definitions then variable is undefined at given location -> signalize error.
  • If there is more than one reachable definition then the variable is dynamic at the given point (see the info bellow).
  • If there is only one reachable definition then recursively follow the chain of definitions upwards and calculate values at definition places until search upwards end-up on a value for which we know that it is either static (with a known value, e.g. a literal number) or dynamic.

A note on static values

In this iteration we are considering a value to be static if it can be proven by DFA that at the given program point its value is known statically and can be only one so that it can be replaced by its computed value at compile time (i.e. we are not doing a more complex range/set value analysis for now).

Please, also note that the static/dynamic property is calculated for each program point. E.g. a variable may be static at some place in the code while dynamic in others. Consider this:

x = 3
... x is static here if not changed
if <condition>
x = 5
... x is static here if not changed
end
x == ? # At this point x may be static if `condition` can be statically calculated 
    # but if not then we can't tell for sure what is the value of `x`.
    # Thus, code pruning may lead to some expressions to became static

docs.title: Terminal analysis
docs.text: Terminal analysis (TA) is a pass of semantic analysis whose responsibility is to determine if a terminal was initialized in current simulation cycle prior to reading it, and to determine if all output terminals were initialized at the end of simulation cycle. Following sections explain implementation details. Readers are advised to read user documentation (Terminal initialization) thoroughly first.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
TA is performed over final program CFG (after function inline and pseudo-while creation). TA is a non-standard data flow analysis. While TA operates over a CFG until reaching a convergence point, transforming input and output sets using transfer and join functions, join functions are not context free. Alternate paths not directly encoded in CFG can be examined in case of loops.

Transfer functions change terminal initialization state for a particular variable used on the LHS of an assignment.

Join functions merge results from multiple incoming paths. Different join nodes have different join functions.

As for all DFA style analyses, proving algorithm termination is necessary. TA is proven to terminate because all rules for DFA termination are satisfied:

  1. Terminal initialization state is a finite height semilattice. While two join operators are defined, set of values has a total order in both cases.
  2. Transfer functions are monotone. Transfer functions always update initialization state to a greater or equal value. Terminal cannot be uninitialized. Terminal, when once fully initialized, cannot become partially initialized until a join node is reached.

docs.title: Terminal initialization state
docs.text:
Terminal initialization state is held in a map-like structure that holds pairs of terminals with their corresponding initialization state. Initialization state of a terminal at a certain point in CFG can be either NONE, PARTIAL or FULL.

NONE denotes that terminal is not initialized on all incoming paths. PARTIAL denotes that in the case of tensor terminals, terminal is initialized on all incoming paths, but that it is not proven that all indices were initialized. FULL denotes that terminal is initialized on all incoming paths.

Following tables show definitions of two supported join operators. Both join operators are commutative and associative, so the selection of shown combinations is reduced. Note that when multiple join operators are used for a complex join function such as the end of for loop, special care about operator precedence must be taken.

operand operand intersect NONE NONE NONE NONE PARTIAL NONE NONE FULL NONE PARTIAL PARTIAL PARTIAL PARTIAL FULL PARTIAL FULL FULL FULL

operand operand union NONE NONE NONE NONE PARTIAL PARTIAL NONE FULL FULL PARTIAL PARTIAL PARTIAL PARTIAL FULL FULL FULL FULL FULL

docs.title: CFG traversal
docs.text: Following sections describe how different CFG structures are traversed and corresponding transfer and join operations that are performed.

docs.title: Beginning of a simulation cycle
docs.text: dummy_global is considered as the beginning of a simulation cycle. New terminal initialization state object is created with state set to NONE for all terminals. State is propagated directly through nodes that do not have associated transfer function. Otherwise, appropriate transfer function is applied.

docs.title: Assignment statements
docs.text:
Assignment statements are the only CFG nodes that have an associated transfer function. If assignment to a terminal is performed, terminal initialization state is updated. Otherwise, no action is performed.

Variable assignment (x = ...) sets terminal initialization state to FULL. Tensor assignment (x[index] = ...) sets terminal initialization state to PARTIAL, but only if former value was NONE. Once state is set to FULL it cannot become PARTIAL until a join node is reached.

docs.title: Shorthand assignment statements
docs.text: Shorthand assignment is considered as a special case, as it incorporates both a read and a write of the target variable. SA does not alter terminal initialization state, as that state depends on the previous state of the target variable. Errors and warnings are reported per rules for terminal reads.

docs.title: Jump statements
docs.text:
Initialization state of a terminal remains unchanged between two points that do not have any jumps and variable assignments. If jumps are present, possible paths to the point after the jump are joined using intersect operator. If no such path exists, code is considered unreachable and TA errors and warnings are not reported.

Warning

Currently, no warnings are raised for unreachable code. This will be implemented in the future.

docs.title: If statement
docs.text: After if statement, terminal initialization of all entry paths to JOIN_IF node is joined using intersect operator. Using intersect enables finding terminals that are initialized on all possible paths.

docs.title: For statement
docs.text:
After for statement not proven to be executed at least once, terminal state is the same as before the loop.

After for statement proven to be executed at least once, new state is calculated in two steps. Output state from all possible loop exits is joined using intersect operator. Loop exit state is then joined with the state before the loop using union operator.

docs.title: While statement
docs.text: As it cannot be proven in the general case that while loop was executed at least once, terminal initialization state is the same as before the loop.

docs.title: Guard blocks
docs.text: Guard blocks behave as ordinary statement blocks, as unreachable branch is always pruned during AST threading.

docs.title: Function call
docs.text: All function calls are inlined in CFG. Every function call is considered as a separate context that both observes potentially different initialization state and that alters current initialization state. Function calls alter initialization state present before the function call. After function call, intialization state of all possible function exits is joined using intersect operator.

docs.title: Final analysis
docs.text: After TA has converged, annotated CFG is analyzed to see if rules are satisfied.

docs.title: Terminal reads
docs.text:
To analyze whether all reads are valid, CFG must be traversed again. Note that while CFG and AST nodes are shared, traversing the AST is not sufficient because of inlining. Every function call is considered as a separate context and contains copies of the original AST nodes which cannot be found in AST directly, but are present when traversing CFG. AST when traversed using AST links between nodes contains only nodes originally present in program source code.

Upon traversing variable reference node, if a terminal is referenced, current terminal initialization state is analyzed. Error is raised if reading a terminal with NONE initialization state is attempted. Warning is raised if reading a terminal with PARTIAL initialization state is attempted.

Shorthand assignment is considered as a special case of terminal read, as it incorporates both a read and a write of the target variable. As SA statements are represented as special variable definition nodes in the CFG, they are differentiated from ordinary writes by their associated AST node.

docs.title: End of a simulation cycle
docs.text:
Last node in pseudo-while loop block is considered as the last node of a simulation cycle. END_WHILE node of pseudo-while loop cannot be used for TA due to join operator implementation for while loop. Note that using last node in loop block is possible in this case as pseudo-while loop can have only function exit node as the last node. Entry functions do not support return statement. break and continue equivalent does not exist for pseudo-while loop.

Initialization state of the last node is analyzed. Error is raised for every terminal with NONE initialization state. Warning is raised for every terminal with PARTIAL initialization state.

docs.title: Porting Singal Processing Components from Jinja Templates to TML - Workflow
docs.text:
The following document describes the workflow of porting the existing Signal Processing components implemented using Jinja templates to Typhoon Modelling Language (TML). A simple constant component described with the src_constant.template file serves as an example.

The first step assumes designing a model containing the targeted component using Typhoon Schematic Editor. As Figure 1 depicts, the model contains only a constant component connected to a probe component Probe1. After validating and compiling the model, one chooses an option to load the model to a Virtual HIL (Load Model to Virtual HIL in Figure 2) and examines it using HIL SCADA.

Figure 3 shows the SCADA panel containing a gauge component inspecting the value of Probe1 output in real-time. The gauge shows the value 23 set by the TSE model shown in Figure 1.

In a more general case, one can inspect the model's behavior in real-time using SCADA and use the inspected values as a reference while validating the future TML implementation of components.

To implement the component in TML, one creates a .tml file with the same name as .template file (src_constant.tml in this case) and places it underneath t_sw/thcc/typhoon/lib_parts/components/tml folder. The following listing presents the trivial implementation of the component in the src_constant.tml file. It shows how the component copies a property's value to the output terminal.

# Listing 1 - src_constant.tml contains implementation of the constant component.
fn output_fnc():
    t.out = p.value
end

To validate the implementation, restart Typhoon Schematic Editor, open the previous model (Figure 1), recompile it and start the simulation in SCADA. If the output looks the same as Figure 2 shows, then the implementation seems correct.

Further validation would require running standalone integration tests present in the t_sw/tests/20_standalone/ folder. One should position itself in the child folder corresponding to the component recently reimplemented in TML (436_constant_source in this case) and run the tests with the command like the following: pytest --vdev=HIL604 --vcfg=1. If the implementation is correct, there should be no failing tests, as Listing 2 shows.

# Listing 2 - Running standalone integration tests for the constant component 
cd t_sw/tests/20_standalone/436_constant_source
pytest --vdev=HIL604 --vcfg=1
# Output:
# =========== 15 passed in 193.21s (0:03:13) ===========
